acceptance:
AC1. ProviderTimeout is configurable per-project via .ddx/config.yaml under agent.routing or agent.endpoints — e.g. agent.endpoints[provider].request_timeout_seconds — and the current 15-minute hardcoded constant becomes a default that can be overridden upward (or removed entirely) per provider.

AC2. ProviderTimeout is configurable per-tier via the model-overrides path: cheap tier on a known thinking model (qwen3.6, deepseek-r1, etc.) defaults to a longer cap (e.g. 60 min or unlimited). Cloud chat models keep the 15-min default.

AC3. The idle-read timeout (DefaultProviderIdleReadTimeout = 5 min) is the primary stalled-TCP-socket defense; documentation in cli/internal/agent/serviceconfig.go and the user-facing config docs explain the relationship between idle-read and wall-clock-request timeouts and when each fires.

AC4. When ProviderTimeout fires, the error message names the exact timeout setting and the path to override it (e.g. 'request_timeout=15m exceeded; configure agent.endpoints.<name>.request_timeout_seconds in .ddx/config.yaml').

AC5. execute-loop / execute-bead --request-timeout DURATION CLI flag exists for one-off overrides during debugging.

AC6. Default for known-thinking-model surfaces (provider.type=lmstudio AND model name matches qwen3.6/qwen-r1/deepseek-r1 etc., or some other declared 'thinking' attribute) is bumped to a sensible value (e.g. 60 min) without requiring per-project config.
description:
Source: cli/internal/agent/serviceconfig.go:30
  const DefaultProviderRequestTimeout = 15 * time.Minute

This is hardcoded, not surfaced through .ddx/config.yaml or any flag, and is applied uniformly to every provider regardless of model class. It is set on every service path:
  - cli/internal/agent/agent_runner_service.go:116  ProviderTimeout: DefaultProviderRequestTimeout
  - cli/internal/agent/service_run.go:153           ProviderTimeout: DefaultProviderRequestTimeout

The comment justifies it as 'Defeats RC4 of ddx-0a651925: a stalled TCP socket that has delivered headers but stopped emitting body bytes would otherwise pin a goroutine until the outer wall-clock (3h) frees it.'

But that scenario is already handled by a separate idle-read timeout immediately below, on line 34:
  const DefaultProviderIdleReadTimeout = 5 * time.Minute

The 5-minute idle-read timeout is exactly the right tool for the stalled-TCP-socket case (no body bytes for 5 min → fail). The 15-minute wall-clock cap on a single request is the WRONG tool — it kills any legitimately long single Chat call regardless of streaming health.

Concrete impact observed today (2026-04-29 axon repo):

1. axon-db7a6d0a worker (qwen3.6-35b-a3b on bragi via lmstudio)
   - status: no_changes
   - duration_ms: 1,762,081 (29 min total — one timeout + retry)
   - log: 'WARN provider error, retrying attempt=1 err="provider request timeout: wall-clock 15m0s" delay=1s'
   - Retry consumed another full 15 min, also timed out, no commits produced.

2. axon-80979cb8 + axon-db7a6d0a + axon-ab2e52e0 (3-worker cascade re-launch)
   - All 3 hit 'provider request timeout: wall-clock 15m0s' on the first attempt
   - User killed the run before retries could waste another 15 min × 3 each

Why thinking models hit this:
qwen3.6-35b-a3b and similar 'thinking' / chain-of-thought reasoning models can spend 5–10+ minutes generating reasoning tokens before producing the first body delta the harness considers progress. Streaming output IS happening (so the idle-read timeout would NOT fire), but the harness is counting wall-clock against the entire request. A single hard problem can blow the 15-minute cap legitimately.

Why arbitrary timeouts are not helping:

a) Same value for cloud providers (claude, codex) and local providers (lmstudio, omlx). Local thinking models routinely need >15 min on hard prompts; claude rarely does. Same cap is wrong for both classes.

b) No way to opt out of the cap per provider, per model, per request, per project. /home/erik/Projects/axon/.ddx/config.yaml has no knob.

c) Retries inherit the same cap. A timeout-prone session retries with the same fate. Burns cycles and tokens.

d) The cap defeats the cascade: when a cheap-tier qwen times out, escalation to standard claude is intended — but on a thinking model the timeout doesn't mean 'broken provider', it means 'this prompt is hard'. We then escalate after wasting 15 min, and the standard tier solves the problem in 2. Net effect of cheap-tier-first becomes negative.
title: agent: hardcoded 15-minute DefaultProviderRequestTimeout kills thinking-model requests; not configurable

acceptance:
**User story:** As an operator, I expect the Sessions page to reflect reality — if I ran `ddx work` today, today's sessions appear within seconds. And I expect the feed not to silently break again the next time the agent execution path is refactored.

**Acceptance criteria:**

1. **Session-index source of truth.** A single writer (invoked from `service.Execute` or a thin wrapper that every production agent-execution path calls) appends one line per completed agent invocation to `.ddx/agent-logs/sessions.index.jsonl`. Fields are pointer-only: id, projectID, beadID, harness, surface, model, startedAt, endedAt, durationMs, cost, tokens, outcome, exitCode, bundlePath (relative), nativeLogRef (relative). No prompt or response bodies embedded.

2. **Single-call-site guarantee.** Unit test enumerates every production code path that executes an agent (execute-bead, execute-loop, `ddx agent run`, quorum, replay if still present) and asserts each appends exactly one index row. If a new path is added later without wiring this up, the test fails.

3. **Visibility restored.** GraphQL `sessions` query reads `sessions.index.jsonl`. Running `ddx work` (or any agent-execution path) against a fixture project causes the new session to appear in a GraphQL query within 2 seconds of completion. E2E test demonstrates this.

4. **Body lazy-load.** A new resolver `session(id: ID!) { prompt response stderr }` loads the heavy fields on demand from the execution bundle (`.ddx/executions/<ts>-<hash>/`) or the agent-log path, without reading every session in the index.

5. **Legacy migration.** A one-shot command (e.g. `ddx agent log reindex`) seeds the index from the existing `.ddx/agent-logs/sessions.jsonl` (397 MB corpus on the current repo), preserving historical sessions. Integration test uses a fixture `sessions.jsonl` and asserts the index has one row per legacy entry with all pointer fields populated.

6. **Deprecated-file behavior.** After migration, the legacy `sessions.jsonl` is not read by the server. `ddx agent usage` and related CLI commands (`cli/cmd/agent_usage.go:197,246,348`, `cli/cmd/status.go:179`) are updated to read from the index too, verified by their existing tests.

7. **Perf sanity.** The GraphQL `sessions` list resolves in ≤100ms p95 on a fixture with 10,000 index entries; current behavior times out or OOMs. Benchmark captured in bead notes as baseline + after-fix.

8. **UI note.** If there is a gap in the index (e.g. the 2026-04-19 → 2026-04-22 gap on the current repo, assuming backfill from executions/agent-logs is not feasible), the Sessions page surfaces a single-line banner "No sessions recorded between X and Y" so the gap is not silently invisible. If backfill is feasible, do it instead and skip the banner.

9. **Playwright e2e.**
   - Seeds a fixture project with `.ddx/executions/` bundles from both before and after a simulated gap.
   - Runs the migration command.
   - Opens `/sessions`, asserts the most recent session timestamp matches the latest bundle, asserts a clicked row lazy-loads prompt/response from the bundle rather than from an embedded payload.
   - Asserts the feed updates live (within 2s) after a new simulated session is written.
description:
## Observed

The Sessions page at `/nodes/.../projects/.../sessions` shows nothing newer than **2026-04-19**, three days ago. ddx is definitely still running — `.ddx/executions/` contains bundles from 2026-04-22 (today), `agent-s-*.jsonl` from 2026-04-21, `agent-loop-*.jsonl` from 2026-04-20. The UI simply cannot see them.

## Root cause

The Sessions GraphQL resolver reads exclusively from `.ddx/agent-logs/sessions.jsonl` (`cli/internal/server/state_graphql.go:504-516`). That file stopped being appended on 2026-04-19 19:46.

The only writer is `Runner.logSession` (`cli/internal/agent/runner.go:847-914`). It is called from four sites today:

- `cli/internal/agent/runner.go:229` (Runner.Run — legacy)
- `cli/internal/agent/agent_runner_service.go:216`
- `cli/internal/agent/claude_stream.go:516`
- `cli/internal/agent/virtual.go:206`

Production execute-bead / execute-loop no longer goes through any of them. The Runner-to-service refactor series (`5b933103`, `280f58e0`, `74eceda9`, `7cc1eb58`, `e05c785e`, `9f79f037`) routed the common path through `service.Execute` directly and did not carry forward the sessions.jsonl append. The last sessions.jsonl write lines up with the day that refactor set became default.

## Secondary issue (same fix should address)

`sessions.jsonl` is 397 MB for only 895 entries because each entry embeds the full `result.Output` (see `SessionEntry.Response` at `runner.go:879`). Growth is unbounded, there is no rotation, and `GetAgentSessionsGraphQL` (`state_graphql.go:476-490`) reads the whole file, parses every entry, and sorts in memory on every query. Even if we just restore the writer, the feed is fragile and will become a perf problem again quickly — relevant to `ddx-ad0db8fd` (bead detail was also O(N) scan).

## Proposed direction

Do not simply paper over by re-plumbing `logSession` into `service.Execute`. The right fix is:

1. **Decide the source of truth for "a session".** Current candidates on disk:
   - `.ddx/executions/<ts>-<hash>/{manifest.json,prompt.md,result.json}` — already written per execute-bead attempt.
   - `.ddx/agent-logs/agent-*.jsonl` and `agent-s-*.jsonl` — per-run streams from the agent harness.
   - Bead evidence events (kind:cost already written per attempt, see `execute_bead.go:492`).
   A session record should be derivable from a combination of execution bundles + agent-log streams, not from a separately-appended aggregate. The aggregate should be an **index**, not a **copy**.

2. **Introduce a lightweight index.** A thin `.ddx/agent-logs/sessions.index.jsonl` containing `{id, projectID, beadID, harness, model, startedAt, endedAt, durationMs, cost, tokens, outcome, bundlePath}` — pointer fields only, no embedded response bodies. Existing `sessions.jsonl` is deprecated to a read-only legacy file during a grace period.

3. **Write the index from `service.Execute`** (or a wrapper the execute-bead / run / execute-loop paths all share). One place to touch. Unit test guards that every production code path that executes an agent appends exactly one index row.

4. **GraphQL reads the index, streams bodies on demand.** `sessions { edges { node { ... } } }` reads the index. A new `session(id: ID!) { prompt, response, stderr }` lazy-loads the heavy fields from the execution bundle or agent-log file when the user expands a row.

5. **Legacy sessions.jsonl compatibility.** The index can be seeded once from the existing 397 MB file so historical data is not lost, then the old file is not read again. One-shot migration command (`ddx agent log reindex` or similar).

## Out of scope

- Replaying or regenerating sessions for the 2026-04-19 → 2026-04-22 gap. If the evidence exists in executions/agent-logs we can backfill the index; if not, accept the gap and cap it with a dated marker in the UI.
- A full telemetry rework. This bead just restores visibility and fixes the shape.
execute-loop-last-detail: escalation exhausted: unsupported model "minimax/minimax-m2.7" for harness "gemini"; supported models: gemini-2.5-pro, gemini-2.5-flash, gemini-2.5-flash-lite
notes:
## 2026-04-22 — User-directed amendment: shard the index

Per user feedback: a single `sessions.index.jsonl` just replays the same problem at smaller scale. The aggregate needs to be split.

### Revised storage shape

- Directory: `.ddx/agent-logs/sessions/` (per-project, under each registered project's `.ddx`).
- Inside: **monthly shards** named `sessions-YYYY-MM.jsonl`.
- Each shard is pointer-only, same fields as the previously-proposed index (id, projectID, beadID, harness, surface, model, startedAt, endedAt, durationMs, cost, tokens, outcome, exitCode, bundlePath, nativeLogRef).
- Writer resolves the target shard from `time.Now().UTC()` at append time and creates the shard file lazily on first write of the month.
- Heavy bodies (prompt, response, stderr) stay where they already live — `.ddx/executions/<ts>-<hash>/` and agent-log streams — and are lazy-loaded by the `session(id:)` resolver.

### Why monthly, not daily / size-based / SQLite

- **Daily** — produces tens of thousands of files over the project lifetime; ReadDir becomes the cost.
- **Size-based** — requires coordinating rotation with the append; race-prone and hard to test.
- **SQLite** — overkill for first pass; revisit if a single monthly shard grows past tens of MB.
- **Monthly** — ≤12 shards/year, human-readable filenames, date-range queries map trivially to shard selection, bounded shard size in practice.

### Reader behavior

- Default Sessions query loads the **current + previous shard** (covers "recent activity" for any current date).
- Date-range queries map the range to a shard set and read only those.
- Pagination beyond the default window triggers older-shard loads on demand.
- No shard-coordination lock needed: each month's file is append-only and only the current-month shard is actively written.

### Legacy migration

- `ddx agent log reindex` reads the existing `.ddx/agent-logs/sessions.jsonl` once and splits by each entry's `timestamp` field into the appropriate monthly shards under `.ddx/agent-logs/sessions/`.
- After migration, the legacy file is renamed `sessions.jsonl.legacy` (not deleted) and never read again by the server. Hand-removal is the user's call.
- Idempotent: re-running the command merges without duplicating (index row dedup by `id`).

### Revised acceptance criteria (supersedes criteria 1, 5, 6, 7 on original description)

1. **Sharded index writer.** Single shared call site (from `service.Execute` or equivalent wrapper) appends one line to `.ddx/agent-logs/sessions/sessions-YYYY-MM.jsonl`, computing the shard from `time.Now().UTC()`. Test: write across a simulated month boundary and assert two shards exist with the correct split.

5. **Legacy migration shards too.** `ddx agent log reindex` splits `sessions.jsonl` into monthly shards by entry timestamp. Integration test uses a fixture with entries across 3 distinct months and asserts 3 shards are produced with correct contents and no duplicates on re-run.

6. **Server reads the shard directory.** `GetAgentSessionsGraphQL` reads `.ddx/agent-logs/sessions/*.jsonl`, respects any date-range arg, and loads only the shards that intersect the range. Unit test seeds 6 monthly shards and asserts a 2-month query reads exactly 2 files.

7. **Perf on sharded index.** Default Sessions view (current + previous shard) resolves in ≤50ms p95 on a fixture with 12 monthly shards × 10k entries each (120k total). Capture baseline (single-file read of same corpus) vs sharded in bead notes.

(Criteria 2, 3, 4, 8, 9 from the original acceptance list remain as written.)

### Scope clarifier

This amendment does not change the single-call-site writer guarantee or the lazy-body resolver — those remain the load-bearing ideas. The amendment is specifically about **how the pointer index is physically stored**.
## 2026-04-23 — Implementation benchmark and evidence

Baseline: `go test ./internal/agent -run '^$' -bench 'Benchmark(ReadSessionIndexDefaultWindow|LegacySessionsJSONLFullScan)$' -benchtime=1x` measured legacy single-file full scan/sort of 120k legacy-shaped rows with 2KB embedded responses at `BenchmarkLegacySessionsJSONLFullScan-12 1 1731705957 ns/op`.

After fix: the same command measured the sharded default-window reader at `BenchmarkReadSessionIndexDefaultWindow-12 1 8103661 ns/op` for 12 monthly shards x 10k rows each (120k total), with the default query reading only the current + previous shard window. Environment footer: linux/arm64, Go go1.26.2.

Acceptance evidence: `TestSessionIndexWritesMonthlyShards`, `TestSessionIndexShardFilesSelectsOnlyIntersectingDateRange`, `TestReindexLegacySessionsSplitsShardsAndDedupes`, `TestRunViaServiceWithAppendsOneSessionIndexRow`, `TestProductionAgentExecutionPathsUseIndexedServiceWriter`, `TestGraphQLAgentSessions`, `TestAgentLogReindexMigratesLegacyFile`, existing agent usage/status/processmetrics tests, and `e2e/sessions.spec.ts` cover writer sharding, bounded reads, migration, lazy body loading, deprecated-file read removal, UI gap banner, and live refresh.

REVIEW:BLOCK

Missing session index writer: no changes to `service.Execute`, shared execution wrappers, or agent execution paths to append `.ddx/agent-logs/sessions.index.jsonl`.
Missing GraphQL changes: no update to `cli/internal/server/state_graphql.go` for index-backed `sessions` or lazy `session(id:)` body loading.
Missing migration command: no `ddx agent log reindex` or equivalent CLI implementation and no fixture-based migration test.
Missing CLI usage/status updates: no changes to `cli/cmd/agent_usage.go` or `cli/cmd/status.go` to stop reading legacy `sessions.jsonl`.
Missing performance validation: no benchmark or fixture proving 10,000 index entries resolve within the required threshold.
Missing UI gap/banner behavior and Playwright coverage for `/sessions`, lazy loading, migration, and live feed updates.

REVIEW:BLOCK

**AC 1–3**: No `sessions.index.jsonl` schema, writer in `service.Execute`, or updated GraphQL resolver in `cli/internal/server/state_graphql.go`.
**AC 4**: No `session(id: ID!)` resolver for lazy-loading prompt/response/stderr from execution bundles.
**AC 5**: No `ddx agent log reindex` command or migration logic.
**AC 6**: `cli/cmd/agent_usage.go` and `cli/cmd/status.go` are untouched.
**AC 7**: No benchmark captured.
**AC 8–9**: No frontend changes or Playwright tests.

REVIEW:BLOCK

Missing `service.Execute` or shared agent-execution wrapper change to append one pointer-only row to `.ddx/agent-logs/sessions.index.jsonl`.
Missing unit test proving execute-bead, execute-loop, `ddx agent run`, quorum, and replay paths append exactly one index row.
Missing `cli/internal/server/state_graphql.go` update so GraphQL `sessions` reads `sessions.index.jsonl`.
Missing new GraphQL `session(id: ID!)` resolver for lazy-loading prompt, response, and stderr from execution bundles or native logs.
Missing `ddx agent log reindex` or equivalent migration command, plus integration test for legacy `sessions.jsonl`.
Missing updates to `cli/cmd/agent_usage.go` and `cli/cmd/status.go` to stop reading legacy `sessions.jsonl`.
Missing 10,000-entry GraphQL sessions benchmark and recorded baseline/after-fix bead notes.
Missing Sessions UI gap banner or feasible backfill behavior.
Missing Playwright e2e covering migration, latest timestamp visibility, lazy body load, and live feed updates.
title: Sessions UI is blind to post-2026-04-19 activity: service.Execute path no longer writes sessions.jsonl

- New 'Cross-platform invariant' section: ddx creates no symlinks during install; reasoning = Windows compat; plugin source symlinks (in tarballs) are NOT materialized — author's responsibility.
- New 'Migration' section: ddx update --force heals pre-existing projects with home-symlinked skills; stale ~/.ddx/installed.yaml entries are no-op uninstalls; ~/.ddx/plugins/ left in place as inert (user can rm -rf).

acceptance: All three child beads are closed. The exit-gate one-liner above runs end-to-end against a real ready bead on a host with a healthy vidar-omlx provider and produces a non-error closing status (success / already_satisfied) or a meaningful provider-side error — not 'unexpected end of JSON input' and not 'Model not found' 404s for catalog-missing refs.
description:
## Target user experience

An operator declares a local MLX server (omlx flavor) once in .agent/config.yaml:

    providers:
      vidar-omlx:
        type: openai-compat
        base_url: http://vidar:1235/v1
        flavor: omlx

And invokes work with a canonical model ref:

    ddx work --once --local --provider vidar-omlx --model-ref qwen/qwen3.6

Expected outcome: DDx fuzzy-matches qwen/qwen3.6 against vidar-omlx's discovered models (finds Qwen3.6-35B-A3B-4bit), dispatches through the embedded agent runtime, streams a valid response, runs the execute-bead worktree loop, and lands or preserves the attempt with an honest status.

Today this fails at multiple layers. The audit for this epic (2026-04-17) traced the resolution path end-to-end and identified concrete structural gaps between SD-015/SD-023 and the current implementation. A fresh-eyes review (codex + opus, 2026-04-17) pressure-tested the plan and led to the child-bead list below.

## Child gaps (all must land for the target UX)

**Foundation (unblocks everything):**
- ddx-8610020e (P1, bug): RouteRequest has no Provider field; --provider is silently dropped at routing normalization unless it names a harness. AC includes explicit --harness × --provider precedence rules.

**Discovery layer:**
- ddx-0486e601 (P1, bug): fuzzy matcher needs canonical-prefix stripping + case-fold AND per-provider-aware pool variant. Merged from two earlier beads (normalization + per-provider) per fresh-eyes review — same file, same test surface, one coherent change.

**Integration layer:**
- ddx-2f5a2284 (P2, bug): catalog/Profile paths skip discovery; fuzzy only fires for uncataloged pins. Fix adds discovery consultation when req.Provider is pinned. AC includes bare-leaf refs ('qwen3.6' not just 'qwen/qwen3.6').
- ddx-3c5ba7cc (P1, task): tier escalation respects --provider affinity.

**Dispatch layer:**
- ddx-6a5dfe35 (P1, bug): parse failure at ddx-agent ↔ omlx boundary. Re-scoped per fresh-eyes review to require wire-level evidence before blaming upstream — the 'direct curl works' isolation is insufficient to pin the defect.

**Documentation:**
- ddx-e3493010 (P2, task): 'Resolution Path' developer guide tracing CLI flag -> HTTP dispatch. AC includes CI grep verifying file:line citations resolve (prevents doc rot).

**Phase 2 — tracked as siblings, NOT exit-gate blockers:**
- ddx-4817edfd (P2, task): capability gating (context window, tool support, effort, permissions). Tracks sub-bead decomposition; epic's exit gate does NOT require it.
- ddx-0216b966 (P3, task): fuzzy match tiebreak — prefer latest version over shortest-suffix. Inherited quirk; flag + track.

**Closed as invalid or merged:**
- ddx-e59e7fe8 (catalog entries): wrong diagnosis (closed as invalid). Fuzzy normalization + per-provider pool + discovery integration make catalog entries unnecessary.
- ddx-b388f45a (per-provider fuzzy): merged into ddx-0486e601 per review — same file, same test surface.

## Evidence

- .ddx/executions/20260418T005017-ac81ac97/ — attempt 1 (tier escalation + catalog mismatch)
- .ddx/executions/20260418T005052-3aad2cfa/ — attempt 2 (parse failure)
- Audit file:line citations in bead bodies; gap map in this description.

## Exit gate

'ddx work --once --local --provider vidar-omlx --model-ref qwen/qwen3.6' against a real ready bead on a host with healthy vidar-omlx completes with a closing status (success / already_satisfied) or a meaningful provider/model error — not 'unexpected end of JSON input' and not 'Model not found' 404s for refs the discovery layer could have fuzzy-matched.

Phase-2 siblings (capability gating, tiebreak polish) remain open after the exit gate closes; they are tracked separately.
resolution_note: Superseded by upstream agent work agent-1a486c2e (consolidated ResolveRoute) plus the routing capability gating beads (ddx-8610020e, ddx-0486e601, ddx-3c5ba7cc, ddx-2f5a2284, ddx-0216b966, ddx-4817edfd subtree). After agent v0.5.0 the routing engine is consolidated in agent's internal/routing.ResolveRoute with all capability gating in place. End-to-end omlx autorouting works via the unified resolver.
title: Epic: autorouting — 'there's an omlx on vidar, use qwen3.6' should Just Work end-to-end

acceptance: File library/personas/implementer.md exists with valid frontmatter. Body has Philosophy / Approach / Anti-patterns / Sources sections. Philosophy section names YAGNI, KISS, and DOWITYTD by name and includes the "performance claims require baselines" stance and the "favor real e2e over adding mocks" stance. Anti-patterns section lists at least 5 concrete patterns (speculative abstraction, unrelated refactor during bug fix, error handling for impossible cases, comments restating code, performance claims without baseline). Sources section cites at least two external works. grep -E "cli/internal/|docs/helix/" library/personas/implementer.md returns no matches.
description:
Replace library/personas/pragmatic-implementer.md with library/personas/implementer.md. The existing file has a generic tutorial tone; rewrite with real opinions.

Core stances:
- YAGNI — implement only what is specified; no speculative features or "while we are here" additions
- KISS — simplest solution that meets requirements; avoid clever code and premature optimization
- DOWITYTD (Do Only What I Told You To Do) — stop when the task is complete
- Ship tests with code — implementations without a test command referenced in AC are half-done (but: test-first vs test-alongside vs test-after is a workflow choice, not mandated by this persona — see the test-engineer persona for the testing stance; this persona requires tests exist and pass, not a specific methodology)
- Do not design for hypothetical future requirements. Three similar lines is better than a premature abstraction.
- **Performance claims require baselines.** Do not add code "for performance" without a baseline measurement and a clear boundary describing what is being measured. "This is faster" without a number is not a justification. (Cross-reference test-engineer for the measurement discipline.)
- **Favor real e2e validation at the integration boundary.** When in doubt between a layer of abstraction/mocking and writing a real integration test, write the real integration test. The cost of mocks compounds; the cost of a real e2e test is paid once.

Body structure: Philosophy / Approach / Anti-patterns / Sources.

Anti-patterns to call out:
- Speculative abstraction (base classes, interfaces, config knobs for "someday")
- Refactoring unrelated code during a bug fix ("while we are here...")
- Adding error handling for scenarios that cannot happen (trust internal invariants; validate at system boundaries)
- Leaving TODO comments for problems that should block the current change
- Comments that restate what the code does (well-named identifiers already do that)
- Adding a mock or stub when a real integration test would be simpler and catch more bugs
- Making performance claims without baselines or boundaries

Sources:
- YAGNI principle (c2.com wiki, various Martin Fowler articles)
- Anthropic Prompt Library entries for coding agents, if any fit
- Relevant DDx system-prompt content from library/prompts/claude/system-prompts/focused.md (already documents YAGNI/KISS/DOWITYTD)
- Brendan Gregg on performance measurement discipline (baseline + boundary)

Frontmatter:

name: implementer
roles: [implementer, software-engineer]
description: Ships the specified scope, nothing more — YAGNI/KISS/DOWITYTD with tests included, real e2e favored over mocks, performance claims only with baselines
tags: [implementation, shipping, minimalism]
---

In-scope: library/personas/implementer.md (new)
Out-of-scope: Deleting pragmatic-implementer.md (deprecation bead); other persona rewrites (separate beads)

acceptance: ddx agent execute-loop --harness claude --model claude-sonnet-4-6 invokes the claude binary, not ddx-agent; session log shows model: claude-sonnet-4-6 not model: vidar or model: bragi; if --model specifies an unrecognized claude model, the error is surfaced before any bead is claimed; ddx agent execute-loop --harness agent --model vidar continues to work as before (regression guard)
description:
When execute-loop is invoked with both --harness claude and --model <provider-name> (e.g. --model claude-sonnet-4-6), the --harness flag is silently ignored and the embedded agent harness is used instead, routing to whichever LM Studio provider is configured as default.

Observed: started execute-loop --local --harness claude --model claude-sonnet-4-6. Every session in the output showed 'model: vidar' or 'model: bragi' and all LLM responses were from qwen3.5-27b. No claude traffic was generated. The claude binary is healthy (ddx agent doctor reports OK). When execute-loop is started with --harness claude and NO --model flag, the claude binary is correctly invoked (confirmed by claude --print process appearing in ps output).

Root cause hypothesis: the --model flag value is being interpreted as a provider/route key by the embedded agent harness dispatch layer. When the model string matches a known provider name or a catalog entry that maps to the embedded-openai surface, the harness selection silently switches to 'agent' regardless of what --harness specifies. The --harness flag loses the race.

Required fix: --harness must take strict precedence over any routing implied by --model. If --harness claude is specified and the model string is not a valid claude model ID, the loop should either (a) pass the model string through to the claude binary as-is and let it fail with a clear error, or (b) emit a startup warning 'model X is not a recognized claude model; ignoring --model flag' and proceed with the harness default. Silent rerouting to a different harness is never acceptable.

Additionally: when the harness override causes a routing mismatch, the error or warning must appear before any bead is claimed, not after execution has started.
title: execute-loop: --harness flag silently ignored when --model is a provider name

acceptance:
1. With --provider vidar-omlx + config profile with cheap:qwen/qwen3.6, routing resolves to Qwen3.6-35B-A3B-4bit via discovery override even though catalog's embedded-openai surface has a different concrete ID.

2. When the catalog-resolved ID IS served by the pinned provider, no override fires — catalog wins.

3. Bare-leaf refs (--model-ref qwen3.6, no vendor prefix) also trigger discovery consultation when Provider is pinned.

4. When --provider is NOT set, catalog-only behavior is unchanged (no regression in unpinned mode).

5. Routing event records 'discovery_override' with kind + requested_ref + catalog_resolved + discovery_matched + provider.

6. Regression test covers Profile+Provider+Catalog+Discovery interaction AND bare-leaf variant.
description:
## Superseded scope — narrowed per fresh-eyes review 2026-04-18

The original bead framing ('fuzzy matcher collapses provider identity; must match against per-provider pool') is now wholly absorbed into ddx-0486e601 (routing: fuzzy matcher normalization + per-provider pool). Both reviewers (codex + opus) flagged the overlap.

What remains is the ROUTING-LAYER integration work that sits on top of the discovery-layer fix in ddx-0486e601: making the catalog/Profile paths actually CONSULT discovery when req.Provider is pinned. Today, fuzzy match fires only on the uncataloged ModelPin branch (routing.go:193-225). The ModelRef path (routing.go:233-247) and the Profile path (routing.go:248-262) are catalog-only; even after ddx-0486e601 lands, a Profile tier's model_override will still resolve straight from the catalog without checking whether the catalog-resolved concrete ID is actually served by the pinned provider.

## Narrowed scope

1. In routing.go's ModelRef branch: after catalog resolution, if req.Provider is set and discovery is available, check whether the catalog-resolved concrete ID appears on the pinned provider's discovered model list. If yes, proceed. If no, run fuzzy match (via ddx-0486e601's per-provider matcher) against the pinned provider using the ORIGINAL ref (pre-catalog) as input. On fuzzy hit, override the catalog result with the provider-native ID and emit a 'discovery_override' routing event.

2. Same integration in the Profile branch (routing.go:248-262) so tier overrides participate in the override.

3. **Bare-leaf refs** (per opus review): when --model-ref is a bare leaf with no vendor prefix (e.g. 'qwen3.6' vs 'qwen/qwen3.6') AND catalog lookup MISSES (falling into the ModelPin branch), the discovery-consultation hook must still fire when req.Provider is set. Prevents UX asymmetry where 'qwen/qwen3.6' works but 'qwen3.6' doesn't.

4. When req.Provider is NOT set, preserve catalog-only behavior (SD-015 intent for unpinned mode).

5. Emit structured routing events on every override: {kind:'discovery_override', requested_ref, catalog_resolved, discovery_matched, provider, reason}.

## Files likely touched

- cli/internal/agent/routing.go — ModelRef branch + Profile branch
- cli/internal/agent/routing_test.go — regression covering Profile+Provider+Catalog+Discovery interaction and bare-leaf refs

## Depends on

- ddx-8610020e (RouteRequest.Provider field) — routing needs to see req.Provider
- ddx-0486e601 (fuzzy normalization + per-provider matcher) — this bead calls it
notes: Superseded by agent-1a486c2e — single routing engine in agent/internal/routing
title: routing: ModelRef/Profile paths must consult discovery for --provider + catalog override

acceptance: Two execute-loop --local workers started simultaneously against the same project never execute the same bead; the second worker to attempt a claim on an already-claimed bead skips it and picks the next open one; a bead left in_progress by a crashed worker becomes reclaimable after a configurable TTL (default: 10 minutes)
description:
When two execute-loop workers scan the ready queue simultaneously, both can claim the same bead before either has committed its in_progress status, resulting in duplicated work or merge conflicts.

Observed: started two execute-loop --local --harness agent workers (vidar and bragi) against the same project. Both immediately began working on the same bead in separate worktrees. Their output files were in near-perfect lockstep (131 vs 127 lines), indicating they had independently claimed and started executing the same item. One was killed manually to stop the waste.

Root cause hypothesis: the claim operation in execute-loop reads the ready queue, selects the next open bead, then writes its status to in_progress as two separate steps. If two workers interleave between the read and the write, both see the bead as open and both proceed to execute it.

Required fix: bead claiming must be an atomic compare-and-swap. The worker should attempt to transition bead status from open → in_progress using a conditional write that fails if the status is no longer open at write time (optimistic locking). If the claim fails, the worker should re-scan for the next available bead rather than proceeding.

For local workers (beads.jsonl on disk), a file-level lock around the read-claim-write cycle is sufficient. For server-submitted workers, the DDx server should serialize claims.

Additional issue: when a worker is killed mid-execution, the bead may be left in_progress indefinitely. The loop should support a claim timeout or heartbeat so abandoned in_progress beads become reclaimable after a TTL.
title: execute-loop: bead claiming is not atomic — concurrent workers race on the same bead

description:
Per plan: docs/helix/02-design/plan-2026-04-29-artifact-and-run-architecture.md.

Scope of vision update:

(0) PREAMBLE for Core Thesis (canonical wording — use verbatim):

> Software is leverage. Fifty years of practice has shown that there are physics of software that must be respected to produce and maintain systems at scale. The agentic era allows every team to produce systems at scale and makes this even more important.
>
> Generative AI brings its own physics. DDx exists at the seam where these two meet — the fulcrum that lets software's lever do work. Without it, shipping software with agents quietly degrades into prompt-and-pray. The six principles below are the load-bearing claims on both sides.

The preamble REPLACES the existing two-paragraph Core Thesis. Existing paragraph 2 ("Creating documentation and using it as an abstraction... DDx encodes that insight into infrastructure...") drifts into positioning rather than thesis — its substance is captured by principle #1 plus existing 'What DDx Is' section. Drop it from Core Thesis when this bead lands.

(1) Thesis principles — restructure Core Thesis around 6 principles in 3 groups (Physics of Software / Physics of Generative AI / The intersection — DDx's reason to exist). Final formulations:

A. Physics of Software (engineering truths agents amplify):
1. Abstraction is the lever. Multi-level artifact stacks (vision -> spec -> test -> code) with maintained relationships are how intent propagates without being lost. True for human teams; load-bearing for agents because they don't carry tacit knowledge between invocations.
2. Software is iteration over tracked work. Repeated trials over an explicit work substrate — beads, queues, dependency DAGs — is how software gets built. Pre-existed agents; agents make the substrate non-optional.
3. Methodology is plural. Different teams, projects, and problem domains demand different workflows — waterfall, agile, kanban, HELIX, ad-hoc. No tool that bakes one in survives the rest. DDx provides primitives (artifacts, runs, beads) that any methodology composes.

B. Physics of Generative AI (claims about LLM behavior):
4. LLMs are stochastic, unreliable, and costly. Cost-tier ladders, ensemble verification, and 'cheapest model that works' are the operating shape of agentic work, not optimizations.
5. Evidence provides memory. Agents carry no state between invocations and outputs aren't bit-reproducible. The only thing that survives a run is what we captured as it happened. That captured evidence is the substrate for evaluation, trust, debugging, and learning — without it, every other principle degrades to anecdote.

C. The intersection — DDx's reason to exist:
6. Human-AI collaboration is the fulcrum. Abstraction levers intent across the artifact stack, but only collaboration converts leverage into shipped software. Humans supply intent and accountability; AI supplies volume and execution. DDx is the toolkit at the seam — handoffs in both directions, at every level.

Note: #1 and #6 form a deliberate rhetorical bookend (lever + fulcrum = leverage). The preamble primes the metaphor without spoiling it.

(2) Operating principles — keep existing list (Git-native, file-first, etc.) but add one-line preamble: 'Operating principles are the choices DDx makes in response to the physics above.' Update Principle #1 from 'Documents are the product' to 'Artifacts are the product (documents primary, other media supported).'

(3) Artifact + 3-layer architecture changes (per original bead scope):
- Thesis copy: 'documents AI agents consume' -> 'artifacts agents produce and consume'
- Artifact-management bullet: broaden to non-document media + generators
- One sentence acknowledging four-way producer/consumer space (no separate matrix subsection)
- New Design-Philosophy subsection: Three-layer run architecture (ddx run / ddx try / ddx work)
- KVP table additions: 'Multi-media artifact graph'; 'Three-layer run architecture (run/try/work)'; note invocation is upstream

Do NOT touch docs/helix/01-frame/principles.md — that's HELIX engineering decision-guide, separate concern.
title: [artifact-run-arch] update product-vision.md for artifact + 3-layer architecture

description:
Both the interactive execute-bead path (ddx-14c0e790) and server execute-loop path (ddx-e437ebc1) need the same setup before invoking gate eval: pin ResultRev, create an ephemeral worktree at that ref, extract governing IDs from manifest, and arrange cleanup. Extract once to prevent drift.

Signature:
  func BuildLandingGateContext(projectRoot string, res *ExecuteBeadResult, gitOps GitOps) (wtPath string, governIDs []string, cleanup func(), err error)

Behavior:
- Read filepath.Join(projectRoot, res.ManifestFile); call ExtractGoverningIDsFromManifest.
- If govern IDs empty: return ("", nil, noop, nil) — no checkout needed.
- Otherwise: pin ResultRev via refs/ddx/gate-pins/<beadID>/<attemptID>, then WorktreeAdd a temp worktree at that ref. Cleanup unpins and removes the worktree.

Also extract EvaluateRequiredGatesForResult(wtPath, governIDs, res, projectRoot) — a thin wrapper around evaluateRequiredGates that also writes checks.json and populates res.GateResults / RequiredExecSummary / RatchetEvidence. Currently inline at LandBeadResult lines 220-245; lift so both paths share it.

This bead lands in isolation with unit tests against fakeExecuteBeadGit. Sub-tasks (interactive + server + backward-compat) all use it.
title: Extract shared BuildLandingGateContext helper for gate-eval before LandBeadResult

acceptance: FEAT-002, FEAT-008, and FEAT-014 define an agent dashboard showing configured providers, availability/health, utilization metrics, freshness, and unknown-state semantics; the design includes a queryable read model and deterministic fixture scenarios for Playwright coverage
description:
<context-digest>
Review area: server agent observability and operator controls. Evidence covers FEAT-002 server surfaces, FEAT-008 web UI, FEAT-014 token-awareness/routing metrics, the embedded-agent progress lane, and the requirement that a host+user ddx-server expose actionable provider availability and utilization state across configured harnesses/providers.
</context-digest>
Define the server-side agent dashboard that lets operators inspect configured providers and make informed execution decisions.

## Goals
- Show configured providers/harnesses, effective routing availability, auth/health state, and current quota or headroom when known
- Show utilization and performance signals already normalized by DDx: token usage, cost when available, recent latency/success, burn estimates, and freshness timestamps
- Support search/sort/filter/reporting across provider, model, profile, status, and time window
- Distinguish provider-native facts from DDx-derived estimates and from unknown values

## Required spec work
- Update FEAT-002, FEAT-008, and FEAT-014 so the dashboard and its backing read model are explicitly governed
- Define the API/read-model fields exposed to UI consumers and reporting surfaces
- Clarify how live provider availability, cached quota snapshots, and historical usage are presented without inventing certainty

## Required implementation planning
- Define sample/fixture data needed to Playwright-test the dashboard states
- Define how the dashboard relates to worker progress, execution history, and routing diagnostics without collapsing them into one page
title: Design server agent dashboard for provider availability and utilization

acceptance: (1) `ddx agent execute-bead <id> --provider vidar` routes to vidar unambiguously. (2) `ddx agent execute-loop --model-ref code-medium` resolves via the model catalog. (3) `ddx agent execute-loop --model qwen/qwen3-coder-next` with NO provider/ref flag errors out with the message described above (regression test required). (4) `ddx agent execute-loop --model qwen/qwen3-coder-next --provider vidar-coder` works correctly and routes to vidar. (5) Unit or integration test covers the orphan-model error case. (6) `go build ./...` passes. (7) `go test ./cli/cmd/... ./cli/internal/agent/... -count=1` passes.
description:
The real bug fix from the vidar→OpenRouter silent-fallback incident. Update cli/cmd/agent_cmd.go (execute-loop) and cli/cmd/agent_execute_bead.go (execute-bead) to accept three NEW flags alongside the existing --harness and --model:

- --provider <name>: passes through to ddx-agent as its `-provider` flag. Selects a named provider from   config (e.g. `vidar`, `vidar-coder`, `openrouter`).
- --model-ref <ref>: passes through as `-model-ref`. Resolves via the model catalog to a canonical target   (e.g. `code-high`, `code-medium`).
- --model <string>: already exists. Currently passed to ddx-agent as `-model` which does a direct model   override. THE FIX: when --model is given but no matching provider can serve it AND no catalog alias   matches AND no --provider/--model-ref is set, DDx must error BEFORE dispatching the agent — "model 'X'   did not match any provider, catalog ref, or model-route; specify --provider or --model-ref explicitly".   Do NOT silently fall back to the default provider (which was how --model qwen/qwen3-coder-next routed to   OpenRouter earlier).

Wire the new flags through RunOptions / ExecuteBeadOptions / ExecuteBeadLoopOptions so they reach the Runner.RunAgent in internal/agent/agent_runner.go, which then forwards them to agentlib provider resolution.
title: Visibility 7: teach execute-loop + execute-bead about --provider and --model-ref, error on orphan --model

acceptance: (1) A projectMiddleware or projectResolver extracts {project} from /api/projects/{project}/... routes and injects resolved ProjectEntry into request context. (2) Document routes (list, read, write, search), bead routes (list, show, create, update, claim, unclaim, reopen, ready, blocked, status, deps), and doc graph routes (graph, stale, deps, dependents, history, diff, show, write) are all registered under /api/projects/{project}/... in addition to existing legacy routes. (3) Legacy unscoped routes continue to work when exactly one project is registered (singleton compatibility). (4) cd cli && go test ./internal/server -run 'TestProject' -count=1 passes with at least one test covering scoped route resolution.
description:
## Scope
Add project-scoping middleware to the DDx server and migrate all document, bead, and doc-graph HTTP routes to canonical project-scoped paths.

## Context
The server currently registers ~50 routes under /api/... without project scoping. FEAT-002 requires /api/projects/{project}/... as canonical. Three worker routes already use this pattern (handleProjectWorkerList, handleProjectWorkerShow, handleProjectWorkerProgress) — follow that pattern.

## Implementation approach
1. Create a projectMiddleware (or helper function) that:
   - Extracts {project} from the URL path
   - Resolves it via GetProjectByID() or GetProjectByPath()
   - Injects the resolved ProjectEntry into request context
   - Returns 404 if project not found
2. Register all document, bead, and doc-graph routes under /api/projects/{project}/...
3. Keep legacy /api/... routes as singleton compatibility aliases that auto-resolve to the single registered project (error if >1 project registered without scoping)
4. Add test(s) that register two projects, verify scoped routes resolve correctly, and verify legacy routes work with one project

## Files to touch
- cli/internal/server/server.go — route registration, middleware
- cli/internal/server/handlers.go (or individual handler files) — extract project from context instead of hardcoding
- cli/internal/server/server_test.go — new project-scoped routing tests

## Files NOT to touch
- MCP tools (separate bead)
- GraphQL schema (already has beadsByProject, workersByProject)
- Frontend routes
title: Add project-scoped route middleware and migrate document/bead/graph routes

acceptance: Backend Go test in cli/internal/server/server_test.go: seeds two projects via state.RegisterProject, each with three beads (open, in_progress, closed), calls GET /api/beads, asserts (1) response contains all six beads, (2) each bead has project_id matching its source project, (3) ?status=open returns only the two open beads, (4) ?project_id=<id> returns only that project's beads. Playwright test loads /nodes/:nodeId/beads, asserts the table contains beads from at least two distinct projects with the Project column populated, asserts filter chip status=open hides closed/in_progress rows, asserts a project filter chip narrows the table to one project's beads, asserts URL query params reflect the filter state.
description:
Same situational-awareness gap as the workers view: when N projects are in flight, the operator currently can only see one project's bead queue at a time via /nodes/:nodeId/projects/:projectId/beads. Add a node-level combined beads view that merges all projects' queues into one filterable table.

Backing data: needs a new endpoint OR client-side merge. The cleanest design is server-side aggregation since clients shouldn't fan out N requests:
- New endpoint GET /api/beads (node-wide, no project scope)
- Returns: array of beads with an extra 'project_id' field on each entry
- Supports the same query params as /api/projects/:project/beads (?status=, ?ready=, ?label=, ?limit=)
- Walks the project registry from FEAT-020 and concatenates each project's bead store

UI requirements:
- Route: /nodes/:nodeId/beads (combined; the existing /nodes/:nodeId/projects/:projectId/beads stays)
- Adds 'Project' column showing project short name
- Filter chips for status (open/in_progress/closed) and project (multi-select)
- Filter state encoded in URL query params so views are bookmarkable
- Auto-refresh every ~10s (slower than workers since beads change less frequently)

Delete-aware: when a project is removed from the registry its beads disappear from the combined view on the next refresh.
title: Dashboard UI: cross-project beads view (combined ready/in-progress/closed across all projects)

acceptance:

- [ ] Decision also recorded as notes on ddx-334d41cb (cmd tier-escalation migration) and ddx-3bf91ebf (server tier-escalation migration) so their migration approach is unambiguous (DDx-internal symbols stay; just decouple from routing.go)

description:
Decide where tier-escalation logic lives (TiersInRange, TierAttemptRecord, FormatTierAttemptBody, AppendEscalationSummaryEvent, ShouldEscalate, ModelTier, AdaptiveMinTier) and update bead scopes accordingly.

Two options:
A. Keep in DDx — these are bead-driven orchestration concerns (per-attempt evidence appending, adaptive min-tier promotion based on cheap-tier success rate). They wrap the per-call service.Execute with retry-on-tier-mismatch. Not LLM routing per se; bead routing.
B. Move to agent — package as a stall-style policy on ExecuteRequest (StallPolicy already enforces some retry; could extend to TierPolicy).

Recommendation: A. Tier escalation is bead-driven workflow logic (consults DDx-side adaptive min-tier from execution evidence), not per-LLM-call concern. Should stay in DDx as a thin orchestration wrapper around service.Execute.

Outcome of this bead: tier_escalation.go stays in DDx; the file-delete bead ddx-1bf6c0df is RE-SCOPED to "ensure tier_escalation.go does not depend on routing.go internals" rather than "delete the file." Update bead notes accordingly.
notes:

VERIFIED: cli/internal/agent/tier_escalation.go currently imports only stdlib + internal/bead. Zero external dependencies on routing.go, state.go, registry.go, or any to-be-deleted file.

Symbols defined in tier_escalation.go (used by cmd/agent_cmd.go and server/workers.go): AdaptiveMinTier, AdaptiveMinTierResult, ProviderHealthTracker, TiersInRange, ShouldEscalate, FormatTierAttemptBody, TierAttemptRecord, EscalationSummary, BuildEscalationSummary, AppendEscalationSummaryEvent, classifyAttemptTier (private). ModelTier itself lives in models.go (also stays).

CONSEQUENCES:
- ddx-1bf6c0df (was "delete tier_escalation.go") is now NO-OP: file stays as-is. Re-scope to confirmation: "tier_escalation.go has no dependency on routing.go internals" (already true).

- ddx-3bf91ebf (server tier-escalation migration) is now NO-OP for the tier symbols themselves; only the deprecated AgentRunnerFactory field deletion remains scoped.

- beadId, artifactId, parentRunId, childRunIds

Resolver reads from .ddx/exec/runs/ (FEAT-010 exec store, file-backed). For work/try layer records read from .ddx/executions/ attempt records.

acceptance:
1. **Root cause #1 fixed:** `attempted[candidate.ID] = struct{}{}` is moved to AFTER the pre-claim hook succeeds. Regression test in `execute_bead_loop_test.go` asserts: with a fake `PreClaimHook` that fails on the first call and succeeds thereafter, the loop processes the same bead on the second iteration (not skipped because of stale `attempted` state).

2. **Root cause #2 fixed:** the post-`Execute` outcome-handling block (lines ~510-862) does not exit the loop on transient `Store.*` errors. Each `return result, err` in that block is converted to: log the error, emit a `kind:loop-error` bead event when possible, set the bead to a short cooldown, and `continue` to the next iteration. The only exception is context cancellation (`ctx.Err()`), which still terminates immediately.

3. **Regression tests for AC-2:** for each `Store.*` call in the outcome block (Unclaim, IncrNoChangesCount, adjudicateNoChanges, CloseWithEvidence on each path, Reopen on each path, SetExecutionCooldown on each path, late AppendEvent), a test injects a transient error from a fake store and asserts: (a) the loop continues, (b) a `kind:loop-error` event is recorded with the failing operation name, (c) `result.Failures++` advances.

4. **End-to-end reproduction guard:** an integration test seeds a bead.jsonl with 3 ready beads. The first returns `no_changes` (executor fake), the second returns `success`, the third returns `already_satisfied`. The loop runs to completion (all three processed) without exiting prematurely. With the pre-fix loop, this test fails on the first bead.

5. **Worker-state semantics preserved:** existing tests that assert worker.State transitions (`running` → `exited` / `failed` / `stopped` / `reaped`) still pass. The fix changes loop *advancement*, not terminal-state classification.

6. **Documented behavior:** `docs/agents/execute-loop.md` (or the closest existing doc) gains a "loop termination" section listing the only conditions that terminate the loop: context cancellation, `Once == true`, `PollInterval <= 0` AND `nextCandidate` returns no candidate, `RoutePreflight` rejected. Explicitly states that Store errors during outcome handling do NOT terminate the loop.

7. **Operator workaround retired:** the README's "if `ddx work` exits early, restart it" guidance (or equivalent) is removed; that workaround was masking this bug.
description:
## Symptom

`ddx work` (no `--once` flag, no `--poll-interval`, default mode) exits after processing exactly one bead instead of draining the queue. Reproduced four times today on `/home/erik/Projects/agent` with `--harness lmstudio` pinned (which sidesteps the unrelated `ddx-c7081f89` server-worker tier-walk issue):

| Run | Outcome on first bead | Worker terminal state | Queue still non-empty? |
|---|---|---|---|
| 2 | `already_satisfied` (`agent-4aa3584e` adjudicated satisfied) | `already_satisfied` / `exited` | yes — 2 ready beads remained |
| 3 | pre-claim hook rejected (diverged branch) | `exited`, attempts=0 | yes |
| 4 | pre-claim hook rejected (diverged branch) | `exited`, attempts=0 | yes |
| 5 | `no_changes` from agent (`agent-6c7a4c11` declined as epic; bead left **claimed**) | `exited`, attempts=1 | yes — 5 ready beads remained |

In all four runs the loop should have continued to the next ready bead. Per `agent/.claude/skills/ddx/reference/work.md`, only `success`/`already_satisfied` close the bead and `no_changes`/`land_conflict`/`*_failed` unclaim and continue. None of these outcomes is documented or expected to be loop-terminal.

## Root cause #1 (verified)

`cli/internal/agent/execute_bead_loop.go:382`:

```go
attempted[candidate.ID] = struct{}{}

// Pre-claim hook: fetch origin + verify ancestry before claiming.
// On error the bead is skipped for this iteration; the loop
// continues (ctx is not cancelled).
if runtime.PreClaimHook != nil {
    if hookErr := runtime.PreClaimHook(ctx); hookErr != nil {
        // ... log + emit ...
        continue
    }
}
```

The bead is added to `attempted` **before** the pre-claim hook runs. On hook failure (e.g. diverged branch), the loop `continue`s but the bead stays in `attempted`. Next iteration, `nextCandidate()` (line 924) skips the still-ready bead because it's in `attempted`, returns `ok=false`, and the loop exits at line 374 because `PollInterval <= 0`. Single-shot exit with `attempts=0`.

**Reproduces:** runs 3 and 4 above.

## Root cause #2 (suspected — needs targeted instrumentation)

Runs 2 and 5 reached `Execute()` and got a real report back. Worker log shows the entry line `▶ <bead>: <title>` but no closing `✓ <bead> → <result>` line (which is written at line 909). That means the loop exited somewhere between line 488 (`Executor.Execute`) and line 909 (the final summary log).

Between those bounds, **at least 16 distinct `return result, err` points** trigger if any `Store.*` operation returns an error: `Unclaim` (711, 700), `IncrNoChangesCount` (717), `adjudicateNoChanges` (721), `CloseWithEvidence` (520, 615, 694, 739), `Reopen` (635, 669), `SetExecutionCooldown` (749, 791, 803, 835, 844), late `AppendEvent` (862). Any single transient store error during outcome handling kills the worker.

For run 5 specifically, `agent-6c7a4c11` is now `Status: in_progress` with `Owner: ddx, Machine: sindri`. The bead was **never unclaimed** despite the agent returning `no_changes` — meaning the loop exited on or before line 711 (`w.Store.Unclaim`) failing. Investigation should look at what error `Unclaim` would return when called against a bead the same worker claimed seconds earlier; possible candidates: tracker-write race with concurrent worker, bead.jsonl re-write during a fetch by the pre-claim hook on a later iteration that already happened (no — pre-claim is pre-claim, not post-execute), or a malformed bead row that fails store-side validation.

Independent of the specific store error: the structural pattern of "any Store.* error → exit the entire loop" is the wrong default. A store error on outcome handling should at most park the bead and continue to the next, never terminate the worker.

## Why this is loadbearing

The loop is the queue-drain primitive. Every observed failure-to-drain in the last 24 hours of operator complaints traces back to this — beads that should have been the worker's second/third/fourth attempt never got picked up because the worker exited after one attempt, and the operator manually restarts the drain (sometimes after a long delay). The visible queue depth is misleading: half the "ready" beads are sitting behind a worker that quietly died.

## In-scope files

- `cli/internal/agent/execute_bead_loop.go` — fixes for both root causes
- `cli/internal/agent/execute_bead_loop_test.go` — regression tests for each path

## Out of scope

- Anything in the `agent` SDK (`/home/erik/Projects/agent`). The loop and all its store interactions are owned by ddx.
- The pre-claim hook's divergence-detection logic itself — that's correct behavior; the bug is how the loop reacts to the hook's signal.
- Adaptive min-tier / tier-walk machinery (`ddx-c7081f89`, `ddx-aec9d68c`) — independent.

## Anti-goals

- Do **not** swallow store errors silently. They must be surfaced (event + log) but should not kill the worker on transient/per-bead failures.
- Do **not** convert the loop's Once/PollInterval semantics — they're correct.
- Do **not** change worker terminal-state semantics (`exited`/`failed`/`stopped`/`reaped`).

title: execute-loop: ddx work exits after one bead instead of draining the queue

acceptance:
1. A test creates base/ours/theirs tracker stages with distinct new bead IDs on both sides and verifies the new command writes a valid JSONL file containing all unique records.
2. A test creates same-bead divergent updates with appended events and dependency edges on both sides and verifies events/dependencies are preserved without duplicates.
3. A test covers an irreconcilable same-field scalar conflict and verifies the command reports the deterministic choice or requires an explicit flag, as designed.
4. Running the relevant Go tests for the bead command package passes.
5. Command help makes clear this is the supported escape hatch for Git conflicts in .ddx/beads.jsonl, not a general hand-edit workflow.
description:
Context: During a 2026-04-30 sync, .ddx/beads.jsonl had a real Git merge conflict after local tracker changes and origin/main tracker changes diverged. The supported ddx bead commands could not resolve it safely: ddx bead import skips duplicate bead IDs and therefore cannot merge same-bead updates from both sides. An operator had to run an authorized structured JSONL three-way merge by reading Git stages :1/:2/:3 and merging by bead id.

Add a first-class ddx bead command for this operation so future tracker conflicts can be resolved through DDx-managed tooling instead of manual scripts. The command should accept the conflicted tracker path or default to .ddx/beads.jsonl, read the Git index stages for that file, merge records by id, preserve append-only events and logical dependency edges, preserve new records from both sides, use deterministic field-level conflict rules, validate JSONL and unique IDs, write the resolved tracker file, and print a concise summary of preserved records and any fields that needed deterministic conflict resolution.

In-scope files:
- cli/internal/bead/ (new merge command implementation and tests)
- cli/cmd/ or command registration location for ddx bead subcommands
- docs or skill references only if command help/docs are generated or maintained there

Out-of-scope:
- Changing bead storage format
- Changing normal create/update/close semantics
- Reintroducing hand-edit guidance for .ddx/beads.jsonl
- Solving non-Git backend synchronization conflicts
title: Add ddx bead merge for tracker conflict resolution

acceptance: Bead read surfaces resolve one current state per bead ID from the append-only tracker; `ddx bead show` returns the latest row; `ready`/`blocked`/status surfaces do not surface stale or duplicate bead states; and regression tests cover repeated updates for the same ID
description:
<context-digest>
Review area: append-only tracker state resolution. Evidence covers the current .ddx/beads.jsonl containing multiple rows for the same bead ID after updates/events, `ddx bead ready` surfacing duplicate IDs, and `ddx bead show ddx-40a722c3 --json` returning an older open row even though a later closed row for the same bead exists in the tracker.
</context-digest>
Make the bead store resolve the latest row for each bead ID consistently across all read surfaces.

## Scope
- Reproduce stale-state resolution across `show`, `ready`, `blocked`, and other derived queue/status surfaces
- Ensure append-only tracker rows are folded to the latest effective bead state per ID before any queue derivation or display
- Preserve events/history while making the live bead view unique and current
- Add regression coverage for repeated updates to the same bead ID

## Boundaries
- Do not change bead mutation semantics or abandon append-only evidence
- This is about latest-state resolution and queue correctness, not reprioritization policy
notes: Fixed in a2db669: append-only tracker rows are folded to latest effective state across show/ready/blocked surfaces.
title: Use latest bead state across show/ready/blocked surfaces

The `git show`/`git diff` call that produces the <diff> section of a <bead-review> prompt passes a pathspec that excludes `.ddx/executions/*/embedded/*`, `.ddx/executions/*/prompt.md`, `.ddx/executions/*/usage.json`, and `.ddx/executions/*/manifest.json`. Verified by reading the updated implementations in `cli/internal/agent/execute_bead_review.go:gitShow` and `cli/cmd/bead_review.go:beadReviewGitShow` — both invoke `git show <rev> -- . ':(exclude)...'` (or functional equivalent) with the documented exclusion set.

2. Regression test: a unit test builds a <bead-review> prompt against a synthetic evidence commit whose diff adds a `.ddx/executions/<attempt>/embedded/agent-fake.jsonl` containing >=20,000 `+`-prefixed lines. The test asserts the resulting prompt is below a fixed token budget (suggest 150k tokens, ~600k bytes) and does NOT contain any line matching `agent-fake.jsonl`.

3. Token-budget guard: a unit test asserts that when a `<bead-review>` prompt would exceed 150k tokens even after the exclusion, the builder elides to a summary stub (per suggested fix #2) and logs a warning rather than emitting the full content.

4. Execution-evidence staging exclusion: `landEvidence` (`cli/internal/agent/execute_bead_land.go`) and `VerifyCleanWorktree` (`cli/internal/agent/execute_bead.go:VerifyCleanWorktree`) stage the evidence directory with a pathspec that excludes `embedded/` (matching the existing `SynthesizeCommit` exclusion at `execute_bead.go:269-283`), OR move raw session logs out of the tracked evidence directory entirely (suggested fix #3). Covered by a regression test that runs a complete execute-bead attempt end-to-end in a temporary repo and asserts `git log -p --follow '.ddx/executions/*/embedded/*.jsonl'` returns nothing (no tracked session log).

5. End-to-end reproduction of the `hx-dd4ab35f`-style scenario: an integration test simulates the nexiq sequence (attempt lands impl commit, evidence commit adds 20k-line session log, review-malfunction triggers retry) and asserts the retry's synthesized `prompt.md` is under the budget cap AND the review call succeeds against a stub provider without an n_keep/n_ctx error.

6. Existing execution evidence continues to satisfy auditability: either still tracked but excluded from review diffs, or moved to a dedicated ref / ignored store per the chosen fix. `ddx agent metrics` and `ddx bead metrics` still surface per-attempt usage/tokens/cost from the evidence bundle after the fix.
description:
## Summary

On retry, DDx synthesizes a <bead-review> prompt whose <diff> section is the output of `git show <resultRev>`. When the prior attempt's landing sequence appended an evidence commit of the form `chore: add execution evidence [<attempt_id>]` that added `.ddx/executions/<attempt_id>/embedded/agent-*.jsonl` (full per-event NDJSON session log) as a tracked file, `resultRev` is that evidence commit's SHA (because `landEvidence` sets `result.NewTip = sha` and the orchestrator copies `land.NewTip` into `res.ResultRev`). `git show` of the evidence commit emits the entire session log diff with a `+` prefix per line. Session logs are 5-50k lines; the retry prompt exceeds every public context window.

## Category / severity / surface

- Category: correctness / resource-exhaustion
- Severity: high
- Surface: `ddx agent execute-bead` retry path triggered by `kind:review-malfunction` (and any other flow that re-synthesizes a <bead-review> prompt from a git diff/show since base_rev).

## Full bug description (verbatim)

On retry, DDx synthesizes a <bead-review> prompt whose <diff> section is the raw `git diff <base_rev>..<result_rev>` of the prior attempt. The prior attempt's landing sequence includes an evidence commit of the form:

    chore: add execution evidence [<attempt_id>]

that adds `.ddx/executions/<attempt_id>/embedded/agent-*.jsonl` — the full per-event NDJSON session log of the prior attempt — as a tracked file. Because that file is *added* in the diff range, the diff includes it in full with a `+` prefix on every line. Session logs are routinely 5-50k lines. One reasonable fix iteration is enough to push the retry prompt past 1-2 million tokens, which no currently supported model can accept — the provider streams back an n_keep > n_ctx error and the retry crashes without a request round-trip.

This is a self-poisoning loop: the "evidence" DDx commits to make attempts auditable is the exact artifact that makes the next attempt undeliverable.

## Reproduction (observed live 2026-04-17 in nexiq)

1. Bead `hx-dd4ab35f` claimed by execute-loop worker (harness=agent, model=qwen/qwen3.6-35b-a3b, provider=vidar).
2. Attempt `20260417T211600-d6a7dee6` succeeded and landed two commits: `cb5e733` (implementation + regression test [hx-dd4ab35f]) and `9a006dd` (chore: add execution evidence [20260417T211600-], adds `.ddx/executions/20260417T211600-d6a7dee6/embedded/agent-1776460560227794836.jsonl`, +20,623 lines).
3. Post-merge review emitted `BLOCK` without a rationale; DDx recorded `kind:review-malfunction` and reopened the bead for retry.
4. Retry `20260417T212928-27f9054a` synthesized `.ddx/executions/20260417T212928-27f9054a/prompt.md` of 6,307,997 bytes; `<bead-review>` block = 6,307,969 bytes; 99.9998% is the diff of the prior evidence commit, 99%+ of that diff is the session log file. Rest of <bead-review> (title/description/acceptance/labels/governing) is ~28 bytes.
5. lmstudio responded: "n_keep: 2,274,074 >= n_ctx: 196,608". Worker recorded failed attempt with zero usage. Same pattern on vidar (n_keep: 1,169,147) and bragi (n_keep: 2,274,074), all running qwen3.6-35b-a3b at max_context_length 262144. Provider/model is irrelevant — prompt exceeds every public context window.

## DDx code-path verification (Claude investigation 2026-04-17)

Relevant files and line ranges in /Users/erik/Projects/ddx:

- `cli/internal/agent/execute_bead_review.go:553-561` — `DefaultBeadReviewer.gitShow(rev)` runs `git -C <projectRoot> show <rev>` with no pathspec. Output is injected raw into `<diff>` by `BuildReviewPrompt` at `cli/internal/agent/execute_bead_review.go:383-384`.
- `cli/internal/agent/execute_bead_review.go:427-431` — reviewer fetches `diff := r.gitShow(resultRev)` then builds the prompt.
- `cli/internal/agent/execute_bead_land.go:540-555` (`landEvidence`) — creates `chore: add execution evidence [<short>]` and sets `result.NewTip = sha`.
- `cli/internal/agent/execute_bead_land.go:428-434` — `RealLandingGitOps.StageDir` runs `git add -- <relPath>` on the entire `.ddx/executions/<attempt>/` directory with no exclusions for `embedded/`.
- `cli/internal/agent/execute_bead_orchestrator.go:250-251` — after Land, `res.ResultRev = land.NewTip`, so `ResultRev` ends up pointing at the evidence commit. The reviewer then runs `git show <evidence-commit>`, which is literally the dump of the session log.
- `cli/internal/agent/execute_bead.go:1135-1164` (`VerifyCleanWorktree`) — also stages the evidence dir via `git add -- <evidenceDir>` and commits `chore: add execution evidence [<short>]`; same no-exclusion pattern.
- `cli/cmd/bead_review.go:162-171` (`beadReviewGitShow`) — mirror of the same bug in the standalone `ddx bead review` command.

Confirmation the bug reporter's claim about `:(exclude).ddx/executions/*/embedded`:
- `cli/internal/agent/execute_bead.go:269-283` (`SynthesizeCommit`) — the exclusion pathspec IS present here; the bug is that the evidence-landing commit path (`StageDir` + `VerifyCleanWorktree`) and the review path (`git show <rev>`) do not share this exclusion.
- `cli/cmd/init.go:19-46` — the gitignore rules DO include `.ddx/executions/*/embedded/`, but `git add -- <dir>` on pre-existing or explicitly-added paths can still stage, and older workspaces may pre-date this gitignore (matches nexiq's scenario where the evidence commit landed the session log).

## Deviation from bug description (minor)

The bug text describes the diff as `git diff <base_rev>..<result_rev>`. The code actually does `git show <resultRev>` (single-rev show). The self-poisoning mechanism is functionally identical: when `resultRev` points at the evidence commit (`landEvidence` + `res.ResultRev = land.NewTip`), `git show` emits the entire `+`-prefixed session log. All suggested fixes still apply.

## Suggested fixes (ascending scope)

1. Exclude execution-evidence paths from bead-review diffs by passing a pathspec to the `git show`/`git diff` call in the prompt builder: `-- . ':(exclude).ddx/executions/*/embedded/*' ':(exclude).ddx/executions/*/prompt.md' ':(exclude).ddx/executions/*/usage.json'`. The same exclusion already exists at `cli/internal/agent/execute_bead.go:269-283` for `SynthesizeCommit`; replicate it in `DefaultBeadReviewer.gitShow` (`execute_bead_review.go:553-561`), `beadReviewGitShow` (`cli/cmd/bead_review.go:162-171`), `landEvidence` → `StageDir` (`execute_bead_land.go:428-434,540-555`), and `VerifyCleanWorktree` (`execute_bead.go:1135-1164`).
2. Substitute, don't reproduce. When a tracked-file addition exceeds a byte/line budget in the diff, replace it with a one-line stub such as: `+++ b/.ddx/executions/.../agent-*.jsonl  (+20,623 lines of session evidence — omitted from review prompt)`.
3. Stop committing raw session logs as tracked evidence. Move `.ddx/executions/*/embedded/*.jsonl` to a gitignored store, or a separate `refs/ddx/evidence/*` ref analogous to `refs/ddx/iterations/*`.
4. Cap the <bead-review> prompt size with a hard budget (e.g., 150k tokens). Elide to summary + log warning when exceeded.

(1) is the minimal surgical fix; (3) is the correct long-term fix.

## Evidence in nexiq repo

- Bloated retry prompt: `.ddx/executions/20260417T212928-27f9054a/prompt.md` (6,307,997 bytes; <bead-review> body = 6,307,969 bytes).
- Successful prior attempt: `.ddx/executions/20260417T211600-d6a7dee6/` (landed cb5e733 + 9a006dd).
- Failing agent session: `.ddx/agent-logs/agent-1776460487823541600.jsonl` (session.end payload has the n_keep/n_ctx error verbatim).
- Bead event trail: `ddx bead show hx-dd4ab35f` — routing, then `kind:review-malfunction summary:BLOCK without rationale`, then `kind:execute-bead summary:review_malfunction`.
title: execute-bead retry prompt explodes when prior attempt committed session-log evidence
