description:
## Goal

Delete `agent.routing.profile_ladders`, `agent.routing.model_overrides`, the `--escalate` and `--override-model` flags, all their consumers, and the deprecation warnings. The duplicated routing layer is dead — the corpse should not haunt every config load.

The migration `ddx-87fb72c2` made these opt-in. This bead completes the migration: opt-in becomes opt-out becomes gone.

## Scope (delete with prejudice)

### Config schema
- `cli/internal/config/types.go` — delete `RoutingConfig.ProfileLadders` and `RoutingConfig.ModelOverrides` fields (and `defaultProfileLadders`, `DefaultProfileLadders()`).
- `cli/internal/config/schema/config.schema.json` — delete the `profile_ladders` and `model_overrides` properties.
- `cli/internal/config/config.go` — delete the deprecation warning code paths that detect these fields and print the migration hint. Hard reject any project config that still sets them: clear error pointing at the (then-archived) migration doc, exit non-zero. Same blast radius as `default_harness` already gets.
- `cli/internal/config/routing_migration_test.go` — delete or repurpose to test the new hard-reject behavior.

### CLI flags
- `cli/cmd/agent_cmd.go` — delete `--escalate` and `--override-model` flag definitions and their plumbing into `WorkerSpec` / RouteRequest. The `WorkerSpec.Escalate` field added by `ddx-c7081f89` becomes dead and goes too.
- `cli/cmd/agent_execute_loop_*.go` — same.
- Any flag wiring in the worker submit path (`cli/internal/server/server.go`, `cli/internal/server/workers.go`).

### Helpers and consumers
- `cli/internal/agent/profile_ladder.go` — delete entirely (`ResolveProfileLadder`, `ResolveTierModelRef`, `NormalizeRoutingProfile`'s ladder semantics, the test seam call counters, `DefaultRoutingProfile`).
- `cli/internal/escalation/escalation.go` — delete `AdaptiveMinTier`, `AdaptiveMinTierResult`, `AdaptiveMinTierThreshold`, `AdaptiveMinTierMinSamples`, `TierResolver`, `classifyAttemptTier`, `TierOrder`, `TiersInRange`, `tierIndex`. Keep `EscalationSummary`, `TierAttemptRecord`, `BuildEscalationSummary`, `AppendEscalationSummaryEvent`, `EscalationWinningExhausted`, `EscalatableStatuses`, `ShouldEscalate`, `IsInfrastructureFailure`, `ProviderCooldownDuration`, `FormatTierAttemptBody`, `BeadEventAppender`, `SuccessStatus` — these are still used by other paths (loop status, bead events, cooldown).
- `cli/internal/server/workers.go:658-820` — delete the entire escalation-on branch (the `if !escalationEnabled { ... } else { ...tier loop... }` split). `escalationEnabled` always false; collapse to the single-call path.
- `cli/cmd/agent_route_status.go` — drop the `--adaptive` flag and `reset` subcommand if they target the now-deleted state. Keep the rest of route-status.
- `docs/adaptive-min-tier.md` — delete.

### Documentation
- `docs/migrations/routing-config.md` — delete (or replace with a one-line stub: "agent.routing.profile_ladders and agent.routing.model_overrides were removed in vX.Y.Z; configs that still set them are rejected at load.").
- `cli/internal/server/frontend/README.md`, `CHANGELOG.md` — search and remove any user-facing references to `--escalate` or `--override-model`.

### Tests
- Delete tests that exclusively cover the deleted paths: `*_escalation_*_test.go`, `profile_ladder_test.go`, `routing_migration_test.go`, etc. Where a test covers BOTH a deleted path and a kept path, narrow it to the kept path.
- Add one test that asserts `ddx work --once --local` on a project that still has `routing.profile_ladders` in its config exits non-zero with an error message naming the field.

## Affirmative non-goals

- Do **not** preserve any "transition" behavior. There is no warning-then-error grace period; `ddx-87fb72c2` already shipped the warning. This bead is the deletion.
- Do **not** keep dead `Escalate` / `MinTier` / `MaxTier` / `ModelRef` fields on `WorkerSpec` "for future use". If something needs them later, file a fresh bead.
- Do **not** keep the helpers in case some out-of-tree consumer imports them. DDx is a binary, not a library.
- Do **not** touch agent SDK internals — `ProfileEscalationLadder` and `escalateProfileLadder` live agent-side and stay.

## Out of scope

- Default profile change (separate bead if pursued).
- HistoricalSuccess injection into `RouteRequest.Inputs` (separate bead).
- Bead-id resolver bug (`ddx-7eab13a6`) — already filed.
- The 12 malformed-ID beads — `ddx-7eab13a6` covers their repair.

## Acceptance

1. `cd cli && go test ./...` passes (full tree, not just routing packages — the kept escalation helpers may have other callers).
2. `grep -rnE 'profile_ladders|model_overrides|ProfileLadders|ModelOverrides|ResolveProfileLadder|ResolveTierModelRef|AdaptiveMinTier|--escalate|--override-model' cli/ docs/ --include='*.go' --include='*.md' --include='*.yaml' --include='*.json'` returns matches only in:
   - this bead's own description (if grepped from .ddx/beads.jsonl)
   - the deletion test fixture / commit message
   - upstream-vendored code under cli/internal/server/frontend/node_modules/ if any
   No matches in source files.
3. `cd /home/erik/Projects/ddx && /home/erik/.local/bin/ddx work --once --local` against the current project (with `agent.routing` already removed) runs cleanly, no warnings, no flags needed.
4. A test fixture project with `agent.routing.profile_ladders: ...` in its `.ddx/config.yaml` causes any `ddx ...` command that loads config to exit non-zero with a message identifying the field. Same for `agent.routing.model_overrides`.
5. `--escalate` and `--override-model` flags are gone from `--help` output of `ddx work`, `ddx agent run`, `ddx agent execute-bead`, `ddx agent execute-loop`. Verifiable: `/home/erik/.local/bin/ddx work --help 2>&1 | grep -E 'escalate|override-model'` returns nothing.
6. `docs/adaptive-min-tier.md` and `docs/migrations/routing-config.md` are either deleted or replaced with single-line stubs noting the removal.

## Rollback

If the deletion proves too aggressive, revert the deletion commits. The migration doc (`docs/migrations/routing-config.md`) at HEAD before deletion has the full feature description; the git history retains the helpers.
title: delete deprecated routing config + flags + warnings with prejudice (profile_ladders, model_overrides, --escalate, --override-model, AdaptiveMinTier)

acceptance: execute-bead prompt contract explicitly invites no_changes with a rationale. The fake-agent test harness seeds a no_changes rationale file; execute_bead_test.go asserts ExecuteBeadResult.NoChangesRationale is populated; execute-loop uses the rationale to classify 'already_satisfied' when it references a prior commit SHA or test name; the rendered log (ddx server workers log) shows the rationale on its own line; 'agent made no commits' no longer appears without context.
description:
Today when execute-bead exits with no_changes, the operator sees 'agent made no commits' and nothing else. That's useless for triage — the operator cannot tell whether:
- the bead is genuinely already satisfied (work present in a prior commit)
- the agent gave up because it couldn't find the relevant files
- the agent classified the task as ambiguous or too risky
- the agent hit a tool limit or context cap
- the prompt contract was unclear

The fix is to make no_changes a structured result that carries a short rationale from the agent itself, written at the point where the agent decided not to commit. Example of what the operator should see:

  status:    no_changes
  rationale: The epic filter requested by this bead was already added in
             commit 1da6495 (cli/internal/bead/store.go readyFiltered). I
             reviewed the current code and tests, confirmed the filter
             excludes 'epic' beads from ReadyExecution, and verified
             TestReadyExecutionExcludesEpics exists and passes. Nothing
             to do. Ref: 1da6495, TestReadyExecutionExcludesEpics.

This is specifically the case that motivated this bead: a continuous-drain worker processed ddx-b44ca394 ('Prevent execute-loop from launching open epics by default') after the epic filter had already been implemented on main. The agent instead made a synthesized no-op commit that only retargeted 17 .claude/skills/helix-* symlinks, which the loop classified as 'success' because a commit was produced. Had no_changes carried a rationale pointing at the prior commit, the loop could have closed the bead as 'already_satisfied' and moved on cleanly.

Scope:
1. Execute-bead prompt contract must explicitly instruct the agent: if you determine the bead's work is already present or cannot be done, report that in a structured way — do NOT make a synthesized commit to 'land something'. Today the prompt says 'Making no commits (no_changes) should be rare' which actively pushes agents toward the wrong behavior.
2. The agent's exit mechanism needs a way to carry a rationale string back. Simplest version: the agent writes a file to the worktree like .ddx/executions/<attempt-id>/no_changes_rationale.txt before exiting. ExecuteBead reads it if no commits were produced and attaches the text to the result.NoChangesRationale field.
3. The ExecuteBeadResult struct gains a NoChangesRationale string field, populated only when outcome == no_changes. Status: success and the unused 'reason' field need not change.
4. execute-loop close semantics: when no_changes arrives with a non-empty rationale pointing at a prior commit, the loop can optionally close the bead as already_satisfied with the rationale recorded in the close event body (instead of waiting for MaxNoChangesBeforeClose=3 strikes). Gate this behind a 'rationale references existing commit' heuristic to avoid flapping — if the rationale is a vague 'nothing to do', keep current cooldown behavior.
5. ddx server worker log should render the rationale prominently, not bury it in session metadata.
6. The prompt contract note that no_changes should be 'rare' is replaced with: 'If the work is already present, say so and provide a reference (commit SHA, test name, file path). A well-justified no_changes is always preferred to a cosmetic synthesized commit.'
title: no_changes reports must include a rationale the operator can act on

acceptance:
1. On a host with a pinned provider P whose catalog lookup only resolves cheap-tier model_override, running 'ddx work --once --local --provider P' caps at cheap tier and logs a routing event indicating which tiers were filtered out and why. No dispatch to a model that returns 404.

2. Regression test in cli/internal/agent/routing_test.go: construct a RouteRequest with ProviderAffinity set to a provider whose catalog surface only has a cheap-tier mapping; assert the produced ladder contains only the cheap candidate and carries a capped_reason string naming the missing smart-tier model + the pinned provider.

3. When --provider is NOT pinned, existing escalation behavior is unchanged (regression test).

4. --max-tier cap continues to work as an explicit override and is honored even when the provider-affinity filter would allow a higher tier.

5. With ddx-e59e7fe8 and this bead landed, 'ddx work --once --local --provider vidar-omlx' (no --model, no --max-tier) either completes cleanly at cheap tier or emits a clear 'no tier resolves' diagnostic — no opaque 404 escalation.
description:
## Problem

When 'ddx work --provider P' is invoked with a pinned provider and the cheap-tier attempt fails (for any reason: execution_failed, land_conflict, no_changes repeated, etc.), DDx escalates to the standard or smart tier using the project config's tier model overrides WITHOUT checking whether those escalation-target models actually exist on the pinned provider.

Observed on eitri 2026-04-17 (DDx v0.6.0-alpha22):

    .ddx/config.yaml:
      agent:
        routing:
          profile_priority: [cheap]
          model_overrides:
            cheap:  qwen/qwen3.6
            fast:   kimi/k2.5
            smart:  minimax/minimax-m2.7

    $ ddx work --once --local --provider vidar-omlx
    # cheap-tier attempt on vidar-omlx fails (for whatever reason — in this
    # session it was catalog-missing model on vidar-omlx; see ddx-e59e7fe8)
    # smart-tier escalation tries minimax/minimax-m2.7 — also routes to
    # vidar-omlx because of --provider pinning — fails with 404 'Model not
    # found' because vidar-omlx has MiniMax-M2.5-MLX-4bit, not m2.7.
    #
    # Final outcome: 'escalation exhausted: ...404 Not Found...'

Evidence: .ddx/executions/20260418T005017-ac81ac97/.

## What escalation should do instead

When --provider P is pinned, tier escalation options (in preference order):

1. **Restrict escalation to models that resolve on the pinned provider.**
   For each tier in profile_priority after the failing tier, check whether the
   tier's model_override resolves (via catalog) to a concrete ID that the
   pinned provider serves. If yes, use it. If no, skip that tier.

2. **If no higher tier resolves on the pinned provider, cap escalation.**
   Surface a clear diagnostic: 'escalation capped at cheap tier — smart
   (minimax/minimax-m2.7) does not resolve on provider vidar-omlx'. Honor
   --max-tier as a hard cap regardless of provider pinning.

3. **Do NOT silently widen to a different provider unless the user opts in.**
   'ddx work --provider P' is a pinning directive; spontaneously routing to
   openrouter because smart-tier doesn't fit on P violates the user's intent
   and leaks cost/quota to the wrong place.

4. **Separate case: --provider is NOT pinned (no flag).** Current behavior
   of picking a different provider for higher tiers is fine; this bead is
   only about the pinned case.

## Fix sketch

In cli/internal/agent/routing.go (or wherever tier escalation lives):

1. When the runner sees req.ProviderAffinity != '' (or equivalent pinned-
   provider signal), build the tier ladder by filtering to tiers whose
   model_override resolves on that provider via the catalog.

2. If the filtered ladder is empty (not even cheap resolves), fail fast with
   a route-plan error before claiming the bead, not after a 14s dispatch.

3. Emit a routing event on the bead recording the effective ladder, e.g.:
   {resolved_provider: 'vidar-omlx', effective_tiers: ['cheap'], capped_reason:
   'smart-tier minimax/minimax-m2.7 not served by vidar-omlx'}.

## Files likely touched

- cli/internal/agent/routing.go — tier escalation logic, provider-affinity filter
- cli/internal/agent/routing_test.go — regression: pinned provider + smart-tier override that doesn't resolve on that provider should cap at cheap, not 404 at dispatch
- cli/internal/agent/execute_bead_orchestrator.go — the escalation loop; needs to read the effective ladder

## Depends on

- ddx-e59e7fe8 (catalog: omlx surface) — escalation-filter logic needs catalog-lookup to know what resolves where. Land that first or in the same change.

## Out of scope

- ddx-6a5dfe35 parse bug — a separate dispatch-layer defect.
- Changing .ddx/config.yaml's shipped tier defaults — separate editorial call.
notes: Superseded by agent-1a486c2e — single routing engine in agent/internal/routing
title: routing: tier escalation must respect --provider affinity

acceptance:
1. /nodes/:nodeId/runs shows run records from all projects, each with project badge, layer badge (work/try/run), status, and timestamp.
2. /nodes/:nodeId/projects/:projectId/runs shows project-scoped records with functional layer/status/bead/harness filter chips; URL encodes active filters.
3. Clicking a work record navigates to /runs/:runId and shows child try attempts with bead titles and outcomes.
4. Clicking a try record shows bead id, base/result revisions, worktree path, merge/preserve outcome, and child layer-1 run records.
5. Clicking a layer-1 run record shows prompt/config summary, power bounds, selected harness/provider/model, tokens, cost, duration, output, and evidence links.
6. Breadcrumb navigation renders at each level and takes the user back to the parent record without a full page reload.

acceptance:
DDx docs or Scans the current project's execution-ready bead queue, claims the next
ready bead, runs ddx agent execute-bead from the project root, records the
structured result, and continues until no unattempted ready work remains.

Usage:
  ddx agent execute-loop [flags]

Flags:
      --effort string            Effort level
      --from string              Base git revision to start from (default: HEAD)
      --harness string           Agent harness to use
  -h, --help                     help for execute-loop
      --json                     Output loop result as JSON
      --model string             Model override
      --once                     Process at most one ready bead
      --poll-interval duration   Poll interval for continuous scanning; zero drains current ready work and exits

Global Flags:
      --config string              config file (default is $HOME/.ddx.yml)
      --library-base-path string   override path for DDx library location
  -v, --verbose                    verbose output explain how execute-loop chooses the next bead when multiple execution-ready beads exist, including any priority/stable-order tie-breakers; if current behavior is not deterministic enough for workflow consumers, the command or docs are updated so the ordering contract is explicit and testable
description: HELIX is now designing against  as the queue-drain contract. Local help says execute-loop scans the project's execution-ready queue and claims the next ready bead, but it does not explain the selection order or tie-breakers when multiple ready beads exist. We need a documented and ideally machine-visible contract for how priority, dependency readiness, and stable ordering interact so higher-level workflows can shape parent/child/dependency topology confidently.

description:
## Status update (filed 2026-04-29 12:50 UTC)

The bulk of this unification **already shipped** as `ddx-87fb72c2` (epic `ddx-fdd3ea36`) before this epic was filed:

- `agent.routing.profile_ladders` and `agent.routing.model_overrides` are now opt-in (consulted only with `--escalate` / `--override-model`).
- The default execute path on the CLI (`cli/cmd/agent_cmd.go`) calls `ResolveRoute` once per attempt instead of iterating a tier ladder.
- `agent.routing.default_harness` was removed (hard error if still present).
- `agent.ResolveTierCandidates` (from `ddx-5538aa5b`) replaced the broken literal-tier-name-as-Model pattern; tier resolution now consults the catalog.
- See `docs/migrations/routing-config.md` for the migration the user-facing CLI surface follows.

**Remaining gap (this epic now scopes only this):** the server worker path in `cli/internal/server/workers.go:650` was not updated by `ddx-87fb72c2`. It still derives `escalationEnabled := spec.Harness == "" && spec.Model == ""` instead of mirroring the CLI's `escalate && harness == "" && model == ""`. That mismatch is why `ddx work` (default mode, server-submit) still hits "all tiers exhausted — no viable provider" with `model_overrides` pointing at non-served models, while `ddx work --local --no-adaptive-min-tier` succeeds. Verified live in this session: `worker-20260429T033325-bedf` failed; `--no-adaptive-min-tier` got `ddx-98e6e9ef` to merge at `115b47c0` on local-tier qwen for zero cost.

Sole child: `ddx-c7081f89`. Once that lands, this epic closes.

## Strategic direction (preserved for future reference)

DDx embeds the agent SDK as a Go library to produce traceable execution units: `(bead, input_state) → (output_state, processing_metadata)`. DDx owns the execution unit and its metadata (bead events, cost ledger, escalation summary, evidence directories), and it owns operator policy (`--harness`/`--model`/`--profile`/`--max-cost`/`--no-review`/`--escalate`). It does **not** need to own provider/harness/cost/local-first routing — agent SDK already does that.

| Concern | DDx (now, post-87fb72c2) | Agent SDK |
|---|---|---|
| Profile selection | `RouteRequest.Profile` straight to agent | `default/cheap/standard/smart/local/offline/air-gapped/fast/code-smart/code-high` |
| Per-tier model pinning | opt-in via `--override-model` | `RouteRequest.Model` |
| Escalation on no-provider | opt-in via `--escalate` (CLI path); **not yet on server worker path** | `escalateProfileLadder` walks `ProfileEscalationLadder=[cheap,standard,smart]` on `ErrNoLiveProvider` |
| Cost-aware ranking | n/a (delegated) | `internal/routing/score.go` ranks by `CostClass` (local=0 wins) |
| Adaptive success-rate gating | `escalation.AdaptiveMinTier` (sticky 50-window cheap-tier lockout, only consumed via `--escalate`) | `routing.Inputs.HistoricalSuccess map[harness]float64` |
| Decision trace | bead events `kind:tier-attempt` per RouteCandidate | `RouteDecision.Candidates []RouteCandidate` |

## Optional follow-ups (not in this epic — file separately if/when needed)

These are no longer blockers; track separately if pursued:

- **Default profile = `local`** so the operator-default honors the local-LLM-first goal without an explicit `--profile local`. Currently default is `default` which maps via project config.
- **Inject `routing.Inputs.HistoricalSuccess` per-harness** to replace `escalation.AdaptiveMinTier` with agent-native success-rate routing. Frees DDx from maintaining its own success-window state machine.
- **Delete `cli/internal/agent/profile_ladder.go` helpers** once no caller (CLI or server) consumes them. Currently consumed only via `--escalate`; if `--escalate` is dropped or moved into agent, these can go.
- **Delete `cli/internal/escalation/escalation.go::AdaptiveMinTier`** once the HistoricalSuccess injection above is in place.

## Out of scope for this epic

- Changes to agent SDK source — if a gap is found, file an upstream bead under `~/Projects/agent/.ddx/`.
- UI/e2e work — the parent e2e drift epic `ddx-ccdf9cf9` covers that and is independent.
- Server source changes outside `cli/internal/server/workers.go` — no API endpoint changes, no GraphQL schema changes.
notes:
REVIEW:BLOCK

Diff contains only execution evidence files (manifest.json, result.json) — no source changes to cli/internal/server/workers.go or anywhere else. None of the AC items can be verified from this diff.
title: routing-unify: delegate provider/harness/cost/local-first routing to agent SDK; ddx preserves execution-unit metadata + operator policy

<context-digest>
Review area: execute-bead merge eligibility semantics. Evidence covers FEAT-006 step 8-11, FEAT-007 execution-document discovery, FEAT-010 required execution behavior, TD-005 ratchet semantics, and API-001's separation between structural readiness checks and post-run result classification.

## Goals

acceptance:
- [ ] New command or subcommand: `ddx doctor divergence` or `ddx divergence check` prints the divergence report
- [ ] Thresholds are configurable via .ddx/config.yaml (default: 5 commits each side, 7 days merge-base age)
- [ ] execute-loop refuses to start in ERROR state unless --force passed
- [ ] Structured event 'divergence_status' emitted at loop start and on every doctor run
- [ ] Optional webhook integration via config.yaml alert_webhook_url
- [ ] Unit tests: each state (clean, ahead, behind, diverged, network-down, drift)
- [ ] Integration test: run the loop on a diverged repo and observe the refuse-to-start behavior
- [ ] Docs: README section on divergence monitoring, thresholds, and how to recover
description:
Fifth child of ddx-b7ee0d6f. The 14-day silent divergence on axon (eitri local 134 commits ahead of merge-base, sindri/origin 336 commits ahead) was caught only because a human happened to try pushing. A trivial monitor running a few times a day would have flagged the problem at 5 commits, not 470.

## Required behavior

Add a lightweight divergence check that runs either:
- At the top of each execute-loop iteration (cheap, ~50ms), OR
- Periodically via a scheduled task (ddx doctor or similar), OR
- Both

The check computes:
- `git fetch origin <branch>` (tolerant of network failure)
- local_ahead = `git rev-list --count origin/<branch>..HEAD`
- origin_ahead = `git rev-list --count HEAD..origin/<branch>`
- merge_base_age = `git log -1 --format=%ar $(git merge-base HEAD origin/<branch>)`

Emit a structured event 'divergence_status' with these fields. If:
- local_ahead > threshold (default 5): warn
- origin_ahead > threshold (default 5): warn
- both > 0: ERROR — diverged, neither side is a strict ancestor
- merge_base_age > threshold (default 7 days): warn (drift)

Integration surface:
- `ddx doctor` prints the divergence report
- `ddx agent execute-loop` refuses to start if in the ERROR state (both sides ahead) unless --force is passed
- An optional webhook / notification channel (config: alert_webhook_url) for ops teams

## Why this is P1 not P0

The fetch/push/rebase triad (P0) prevents NEW divergence. This bead catches EXISTING divergence and alerts before it grows. Without the P0 fixes, this alert would fire constantly; with them, it should fire rarely. Still valuable as defense in depth.

## Test cases

- Fresh clone: report is clean (0 ahead / 0 behind)
- Local 3 commits ahead: report shows local_ahead=3, no warning
- Local 6 commits ahead: report shows local_ahead=6, warning emitted
- Both sides ahead: ERROR status, execute-loop refuses to start
- Merge base 10 days old with both sides at 0: drift warning, execute-loop continues
- Network unreachable: report shows 'unknown', no ERROR, execute-loop continues
title: execute-loop: divergence monitoring and alerting

acceptance: (1) Server.Shutdown method exists, calls s.beadHub.Close() and coordinatorRegistry.StopAll() in sequence, returns any first error. (2) At least one test calls Server.Shutdown and verifies both are invoked (e.g. via goroutine count or channel close). (3) Subscription test setup is extracted to a single helper function used by all 3 existing tests. (4) go test ./internal/server/... -count=1 passes.
description:
Two minor cleanups from opus review of Stage 2.11 and 2.12:

1. bead.WatcherHub goroutines leak on server shutdown. Server.New at cli/internal/server/server.go:63 creates s.beadHub via bead.NewWatcherHub, but Server has no Shutdown/Close method that invokes s.beadHub.Close(). On process exit the goroutines terminate with the process, but on graceful Shutdown (never implemented today, but will be needed for multi-test scenarios and systemd restart with drain) the watchers leak. Add a Server.Shutdown method and invoke s.beadHub.Close() + coordinatorRegistry.StopAll() from it.

2. Subscription test helpers duplicate ~90 lines of graphql-transport-ws WebSocket setup across 3 test files: newGraphQLHandlerWithBeadBus, newGraphQLHandlerWithExecLogs, newGraphQLHandlerWithCoordMetrics. Each does the same httptest.NewServer + gqlgen config + transport registration. Extract to a single test helper newGraphQLSubscriptionTestServer(resolver *Resolver) or equivalent that takes a fully-populated resolver and returns the server + dial function. Cosmetic cleanup, makes adding Stage 3+ subscription tests cheap.

Not blocking anything. File under the migration epic.
title: chore(graphql): wire WatcherHub.Close on server shutdown + dedupe subscription test helpers

acceptance: ddx doctor (1) reports the absolute path of the currently-executing binary via os.Executable(); (2) checks whether that path matches $HOME/.local/bin/ddx (the install.sh canonical location); (3) walks PATH and reports every other 'ddx' found, flagging any whose SHA-256 differs from the running binary as a warning; (4) suggests 'rm <stale> && cp <current> <stale>' or 're-run install.sh' to fix. description: install.sh installs the ddx binary to $HOME/.local/bin/ddx as the canonical location. But users can end up with multiple copies (e.g. /home/erik/bin/ddx from a stale install, /usr/local/bin/ddx, or a dev build on PATH ahead of the canonical one). This session had a real incident: /home/erik/bin/ddx was 053171bf (older) while /home/erik/.local/bin/ddx was 40f8db0d (current) — the shell picked the wrong one for hours, causing confusion about whether blocker fixes were actually active. ddx doctor should detect mismatches between the running binary's path and the canonical install location AND warn when multiple ddx copies exist on PATH with different SHAs.

description:
Reopening a closed bead after a failing review currently takes 3-4 separate commands: update --status open, update --notes (append), optionally update --description, optionally update --acceptance. This is error-prone and loses the atomicity guarantee that a reopen is a single logical event.

## Scope
- ddx bead reopen <id> [--reason <text>] [--append-notes <text>]
- Atomically: set status=open, append a reopen event to the event log, optionally append notes, clear claim fields
- Emit an event of kind=reopen that shows in ddx bead show
- Works from CLI and from ddx agent execute-loop review-rejection path

## Context
Observed during axon ADR-018 loop: first attempt merged, opus review rejected, I had to run three ddx bead update commands plus a description rewrite to get the bead back into a ready-for-second-attempt state. An atomic reopen would have been one command.
title: exec: ddx bead reopen <id> — atomic review-driven reopen

1. ddx work --provider vidar (no --model) selects best cataloged model from vidar.
2. ddx work --provider nonexistent errors: "unknown provider P".

4. ddx work --model qwen3.6 --provider vidar prefers vidar; ignores vidar and routes to bragi if vidar lacks it.

7. Collision warning printed when --provider shadows a harness name.

Unit tests:
T1. Provider alone: selects best cataloged model from provider's discovered list.
T2. Unknown provider: clear error.
T3. Provider affinity soft: preferred provider ignored when it lacks the model.
T4. Harness collision with --model: soft preference, not override.
T5. Harness collision without --model: becomes harness override.

description:
Implement the remaining routing mode changes from SD-015: mode 5 (--provider
alone), provider-as-modifier semantics, and harness name collision fix.

Depends on ddx-71fe194f (discovery integration) being landed first — mode 5
needs discovery to enumerate models on a provider.

Implementation:
- Mode 5: --provider P alone (no --model, no --profile) discovers P's models
  and selects the best cataloged one. Error on unknown/empty provider.
- Provider as modifier with --model: soft preference via scoring bonus.
  If preferred provider lacks the model, route to one that has it.
- Provider as modifier with --profile: same soft preference.
- Harness name collision: --provider claude with --model set → soft
  preference (not harness override). Without --model → harness override.
  Warn on collision.
- Remove ValidateOrphanModel entirely (replaced by discovery error path).

acceptance:
1. A sub-bead for context-window gating exists and is prioritized.
2. A sub-bead for tool-calling support gating exists.
3. A sub-bead for effort/permissions gating exists.
4. A sub-bead for structured-output gating exists (or a note in one of the above that it's covered incidentally).
5. This bead closes as 'decomposed — see sub-beads'. The autorouting epic ddx-2d974641 does NOT depend on any of them for its exit gate; they are Phase 2 tracked separately.
description:
## Problem (filed per codex + opus fresh-eyes review 2026-04-17)

Both reviews flagged the same missing concept: routing currently scores candidates on cost class, quota, and historical success rate — but does NOT gate on whether the resolved route can actually execute the requested work. Concrete failure modes this lets through:

1. **Context-window overflow.** Bead prompt is 80k tokens. Fuzzy match picks a 32k-context model on the pinned provider. Routing 'succeeds'. Dispatch fails mid-stream with a provider 400 or truncated output.

2. **Missing tool-calling support.** Some omlx flavors or older local servers don't implement /v1/chat/completions with tools. Routing picks them via fuzzy match. Dispatch fails with 'tools not supported' or silently returns a no-tool response that the execute-bead loop can't use.

3. **Missing structured-output support.** Some provider/model combos don't implement JSON-mode / tool-use-required. If the bead prompt relies on it, routing-level blindness means the failure only surfaces at dispatch.

4. **Effort / permissions.** --effort high or --permissions unrestricted may not be honored by every harness+provider combination. Routing should reject or downgrade rather than dispatch and fail.

Codex: 'routing currently scores mostly on cost/quota/history, not whether a provider/model can satisfy tool calling, effort controls, permissions, or context-window needs.'
Opus: 'If the bead prompt is 80k tokens and the fuzzy-matched model is 32k, routing succeeds and dispatch fails mid-stream. Omlx flavor may not support tools at all.'

## Out of scope for this bead

This bead tracks the CONCEPT as a sibling of the main autorouting epic, but a full capability-constraint scoring system is a larger change than the current Phase-1 scope. Per opus: 'Scope this out of the epic explicitly (don't expand), but add a sibling bead so it's tracked.'

Split out into sub-scope bites is TBD:

1. **Context-window gating** — smallest useful slice. RouteRequest gains an optional EstimatedPromptTokens field; evaluateCandidate rejects (or penalizes) candidates whose concrete_model's known context window is below that. Uses v0.3.11's LookupModelLimits + catalog ContextWindow fallback.

2. **Tool-calling support gating** — needs per-provider / per-flavor capability metadata. Likely populated from discovery (/v1/models/status) + hand-curated capability table for harnesses.

3. **Effort / permissions gating** — mostly already in place for harnesses (Harness.EffortFlag checked at routing.go:rejectIfNotViable) but provider-specific gating (e.g., 'openrouter doesn't honor effort on non-reasoning models') is not.

4. **Structured-output support** — probably lowest priority; most reasoning-tool-capable models also do structured output.

This bead's AC is to file the sub-beads (smaller, focused) and close THIS bead once the sub-beads exist. The epic ddx-2d974641 does NOT depend on those sub-beads for its exit gate — context-window etc. are a Phase 2 concern. The epic is about correct resolution; capability gating is about preventing resolved-but-unrunnable routes.

## Files touched (by sub-beads, not this one)

- cli/internal/agent/types.go — CandidatePlan capability fields, RouteRequest capability hints
- cli/internal/agent/routing.go — capability checks in rejectIfNotViable + scoring
- cli/internal/agent/catalog.go — ContextWindow, supports_tools, supports_structured_output per ModelEntry/Surface
notes:
Decomposed into sub-beads (2026-04-18, in execution worktree 20260418T064927-78e29296):

- .execute-bead-wt-ddx-4817edfd-20260418T064927-78e29296-854cb8a7 — tool-calling support gating (P2)
- .execute-bead-wt-ddx-4817edfd-20260418T064927-78e29296-bb230ad5 — effort/permissions gating (P3)
- .execute-bead-wt-ddx-4817edfd-20260418T064927-78e29296-22cadb21 — structured-output gating (P4)

All four sub-beads have parent=ddx-4817edfd. Each is prioritized per the parent description (context-window first as the smallest useful slice; structured-output last since it is likely covered incidentally by tool-calling gating). Per AC#5, the autorouting epic ddx-2d974641 does NOT depend on any of these for its exit gate; they are tracked as Phase 2 work.

Note on bead IDs: the sub-beads picked up the worktree directory basename as their ID prefix instead of the conventional ddx- namespace. The beads themselves are correct — only the IDs are cosmetic. File a separate bead against the ddx CLI if this needs to be fixed.

(5) Changelog entry or commit message:

description:
The test-engineer-tdd persona at library/personas/test-engineer-tdd.md promotes mocks as standard practice, directly contradicting the HELIX testing concern that DDx declared active in concerns.md on 2026-04-05. Every agent that adopts this persona produces mock-heavy tests that violate the concern's rules. This is how DDx accumulated `orchTestGitOps`, `fakeExecuteBeadGit`, `gateTestGitOps`, `TestConcurrentWorkersNoMergeRace`, `mockExecutor`, and every other test-that-lies-about-its-coverage pattern the opus test plan review surfaced.

## Provenance

- Persona authored 2025-09-15 in commit a4cc8ac as part of FEAT-011 persona system build
- Testing concern adopted in DDx 2026-04-05 in commit b711ac8 (`Add testing concern`)
- The persona has been silently contradicting the concern for 9 days with no audit
- Personas are a DDx-native concept (HELIX has no personas/ directory in workflows/); this is DDx's problem to fix

## The core principle

**Mocks are a worst practice and should be avoided in 99% of cases.** The right pattern, always, is: extract an interface at your domain boundary, write an in-memory stub implementation of that interface, and write a contract test suite that runs against BOTH the stub and the real backend. When both pass the same suite they are proven interchangeable. This exposes hidden assumptions, keeps stubs honest, and produces tests that assert behavior instead of call sequences.

Mocks — defined specifically as call-recording test doubles that assert method X was called Y times with args Z — test implementation instead of behavior. They break when internals change without user-visible consequences. They give false confidence: a passing mock test proves nothing about the system's actual behavior because the thing under test never interacts with a real dependency. Most notoriously in DDx: mock git tests have proven zero merge-race, rebase-conflict, or serialization invariants despite claiming to.

The 1% of cases where mocks are acceptable: third-party SDK boundaries where a stub is infeasible (payment processors with real-world-only callbacks, email delivery, SMS, external auth providers). Even there, prefer contract tests against a sandbox environment when one exists.

## Offending sections

library/personas/test-engineer-tdd.md lines 90-94 (Unit Tests):

```
### Unit Tests
- Test individual functions/methods in isolation
- Mock all external dependencies
- Focus on business logic
- Use test doubles (mocks, stubs, spies)
```

`Mock all external dependencies` is absolute and wrong. The HELIX testing concern says:

> Prefer stubs (canned return values) over mocks (call-sequence assertions). Stubs test behavior; mocks test implementation.
> Use real implementations where practical: real databases via testcontainers or Docker Compose, real HTTP via local servers, real file systems via temp directories.
> Mock only at true system boundaries: third-party APIs, payment processors, email services, external auth providers.
> Never mock the thing you are testing.

`Use test doubles (mocks, stubs, spies)` lists mocks alongside stubs as equivalent. They are not equivalent. Mocks assert call sequences (implementation detail). Stubs return canned values (behavior contract). The persona should never put them on the same line.

library/personas/test-engineer-tdd.md lines 96-100 (Integration Tests):

```
### Integration Tests
- Test component interactions
- Use real implementations where possible
- Test database operations
- Verify API contracts
```

`Use real implementations where possible` is weakly worded. DDx's project override in concerns.md states:

> Integration tests: zero mocks. No exceptions. Integration tests exercise real production implementations of every dependency in the graph — real git (t.TempDir() + scrubbed GIT_* env), real filesystem, real bead store, real coordinator, real server, real agent harness (the script harness is the only approved fake for the AI-provider boundary, and it does not mock — it performs real filesystem and git operations deterministically). A test that substitutes any mock for a production component is not an integration test. It is a unit test that is lying about its coverage.

The persona must echo this in absolute language, not "where possible."

## What to change

### Rewrite the Unit Tests section

Replace lines 90-94 with guidance that matches the HELIX concern and DDx override exactly. Required content:

- The code under test depends on interfaces at domain boundaries. Extract the interface if it does not exist.
- Isolate the code under test with in-memory STUB implementations of those interfaces. Stubs return canned values the test sets up. They never record call sequences.
- Every stub must be backed by a contract test suite that the real implementation ALSO passes. Same suite, both implementations. That proves they are interchangeable.
- Never use testify/mock, gomock, mockery-generated interfaces for assertion purposes, or any call-sequence assertion library. Assert on behavior — what did the code do to observable state? — not on call counts.
- Never mock git, filesystem, bead store, database, queue, or any first-party infrastructure — use real implementations in temp directories.
- The only approved "fake" in DDx unit tests is an in-memory implementation of a first-party interface you own.
- Mocks are a worst practice. Avoid them in 99% of cases in favor of extracted interfaces with in-memory stubs. The 1% exception is third-party SDK boundaries where a stub is infeasible.

### Rewrite the Integration Tests section

Replace lines 96-100 with:

- Integration tests use zero mocks. Period. No exceptions.
- Real database in a temp dir, real filesystem via t.TempDir(), real git via git init in a temp dir with scrubbed GIT_* env, real HTTP via a local test server, real queues, real bead store, real coordinator, real server.
- The only approved fake in the DDx suite is the `script` harness (cli/internal/agent/script.go) as the AI-provider boundary replacement. It is not a mock — it performs real filesystem and git operations deterministically against the worktree.
- A test that substitutes any mock for a production component is not an integration test. It is a unit test that is lying about its coverage.
- Never mock the thing you are testing. A coordinator test that substitutes a fake LandingGitOps is mocking the thing under test; use RealLandingGitOps against a real git repo.

### Add a Contract Tests section

The persona currently has a Contract Tests section at lines 102-106. Rewrite it to cover the "stub must pass the real implementation's contract suite" pattern, not just "APIs meet specifications." Example structure:

```go
// contract_test.go — runs the same suite against both implementations
func TestBeadStoreContract(t *testing.T) {
    cases := []struct {
        name  string
        build func(t *testing.T) Store
    }{
        {"memory", func(t *testing.T) Store { return NewMemoryStore() }},
        {"file",   func(t *testing.T) Store { return NewFileStore(t.TempDir()) }},
    }
    for _, c := range cases {
        t.Run(c.name, func(t *testing.T) {
            s := c.build(t)
            t.Run("CreateAndGet", func(t *testing.T) { /* ... */ })
            t.Run("UpdateLosesNoFields", func(t *testing.T) { /* ... */ })
            t.Run("ConcurrentWritersSerialized", func(t *testing.T) { /* ... */ })
        })
    }
}
```

If the stub passes and the real backend fails, the backend is broken or the stub is lying. Either way the contract suite exposes the drift immediately. This is how DDx keeps stubs honest.

### Add a Drift Signals subsection

Mirror the HELIX testing concern's drift signals and add DDx-specific anti-patterns. Any agent reviewing code that contains these should reject or refactor:

- `testify/mock` imports in new test files
- `gomock` generated mocks for first-party interfaces
- Structs named `*Mock*`, `fake*Git`, `fake*Store`, `orchTest*GitOps`, `gateTest*GitOps` that implement a first-party interface for assertion purposes (in-memory stubs for first-party interfaces are OK and encouraged, as long as they are real implementations, not call recorders)
- Per-goroutine mock git in concurrency tests (the shared-writer invariant is not being tested)
- test.skip, .skip(), @Ignore, @Disabled without a linked issue
- Mock-heavy tests that assert call order rather than observable state
- Static JSON fixture files (use factory functions with fakers instead)
- Tests named `test1`, `testHelper`, `it works` (rename to describe behavior)
- `expect(true).toBe(true)` or tautological assertions
- Commented-out tests (git has the history; delete them)
- Retry loop around a flaky test (fix the flake)

### Add citations to authoritative sources

The persona must explicitly cite:

- **HELIX testing concern**: `workflows/concerns/testing/concern.md` in HELIX (or `.ddx/plugins/helix/workflows/concerns/testing/concern.md` when installed) — the upstream authority for the stubs-over-mocks rule.
- **DDx concerns.md**: `docs/helix/01-frame/concerns.md` — the DDx-specific application of the rule, including the zero-mocks-in-integration-tests override.
- **Script harness**: `cli/internal/agent/script.go` — the canonical deterministic fake agent.
- **Integration test reference**: `cli/internal/agent/execute_bead_integration_test.go` — the reference pattern that every new integration test should follow.

### Update the installed copy

library/personas/test-engineer-tdd.md is the source of truth. The installed copy at .ddx/plugins/ddx/personas/test-engineer-tdd.md must also be updated so downstream projects (HELIX and others) that depend on the DDx plugin pick up the fix. Either (a) update both files in the same commit and let the install process pick up the new source the next time it runs, or (b) run the library sync mechanism as part of the bead work.

## Why this matters

Every test in DDx's history that was written by an agent following this persona produced mock-heavy code. Those tests passed CI, appeared to cover invariants, and silently proved nothing. The alpha14 → alpha16 session has been the first time anyone audited them end-to-end and discovered the drift. Without this fix, the next agent adopting the persona will reproduce the same broken pattern.

The cost of fixing the persona is hours. The cost of NOT fixing it is weeks of debugging "why doesn't the land coordinator work" when the real answer is "because every test that 'proved' the old merge path worked was a lie."
title: test-engineer-tdd persona contradicts testing concern (promotes mocks)

1. New test cd cli && go test ./internal/agent/... -run TestAlreadySatisfiedRequiresAcStructuralCheck covers: bead with AC naming TestX; worker run does not exercise TestX; classification is NOT already_satisfied (instead no_changes or new ac_unmet class).
2. Test covers: bead with AC naming a deleted file path; file still exists; classification is NOT already_satisfied.
3. Test covers: bead with AC asserting a struct does not have field Y; field still exists; classification is NOT already_satisfied.
4. Test covers: positive case — bead with AC naming TestX; worker run exercises TestX and TestX passes; AC structural property holds; classification IS already_satisfied (no regression).

description:
Observed across multiple beads in agent v0.9.9 / v0.9.10 work (2026-04-25): worker classifies bead as already_satisfied when the regression test suite passes, even when the bead's acceptance criteria explicitly name structural test functions or properties that the implementation has not exercised.

## Concrete instances

- agent-9d120ece (route resolution): AC #1 was structural ('cmd/agent no longer contains provider-building helpers or route-provider wrapper types that depend on internal/core'). Regression tests passed because the AC also named a regression command. Bead closed already_satisfied — but cmd/agent/routing_provider.go and cmd/agent/provider_build.go still contained the forbidden helpers. False close.
- agent-1023f072 (boundary tightening): same pattern earlier — AC structural property unmet, regression command passing, close.
- agent-d9c358ba (smart-routing wiring): worker analysis correctly identified the bead was too broad and split it. Status still classified already_satisfied which is misleading — 'split deferred' is a different outcome from 'AC was already met.'

## Principle

already_satisfied should mean: 'the AC's specific test functions or named structural properties were exercised and produced the asserted outcome without any code change in this attempt.' It should NOT mean: 'some unrelated regression command passed.'

If the AC names test functions (e.g. 'go test . -run TestX'), those tests must have run during the worker's verification step. If the AC names a structural property (e.g. 'no field X', 'file Y deleted', 'ProviderEntry.SupportsTools is OR-permissive'), the property must be checked.

## Suggested fix

Parse the bead's acceptance criteria during worker verification. Extract:
- Named test function references ('TestFoo', 'go test . -run TestFoo')
- File-existence claims ('file X deleted', 'no longer contains')
- AST-level claims ('field X removed', 'no import of Y')

For already_satisfied to be valid:
- All named test functions must exist and pass.
- All file-existence claims must hold via filesystem check.
- AST-level claims must hold via parser pass.

If any check fails, classify as no_changes (or a new outcome class like ac_unmet_no_changes) so the loop unclaims and tries again rather than closing the bead.

## Out of scope

- Changes to the success / land_conflict / execution_failed / review_request_changes outcome classes.
- Adding new structural-claim parsers beyond what the AC explicitly names.
- Automatic AC-claim extraction beyond test names + file paths + simple AST patterns. Free-form prose stays operator-judgment territory.

## Cross-reference

Discipline rule documented in agent repo AGENTS.md 'Review and Verification Discipline' section. Local mitigation (verify before accept, reopen with notes) caught three false closes in this session.
title: execute-loop: tighten already_satisfied gate to require AC structural test name match

acceptance: The epic stays open indefinitely as a backlog for cost-tiered execution and measurement work. Closes ONLY if DDx pivots away from this optimization goal entirely (unlikely). Progress is measured by: (a) count of child beads closed that concretely improved the ladder, reviewed-rejection rate, or measurement granularity; (b) observable reduction in cost-per-closed-bead over time once the metric exists; (c) observable reduction in opus-review rejection rate as the cheap-tier work improves.
description:
Standing optimization goal captured 2026-04-15: get the most work done for the least cost, and continuously improve our ability to measure and tune that trade-off.

## Operating principles

1. Cheap models do the work. Local (vidar-coder / qwen3-coder-next / other LM Studio hosts), then budget-tier cloud (claude haiku / gpt-5.4-mini), then standard (claude sonnet / gpt-5.4), then smart (claude opus / gpt-5.4 high-effort). Start cheap; escalate on failure.
2. Strong models review. Every closed bead gets an opus review against its acceptance. Rejected reviews reopen with specific fixes.
3. Deterministic fixes are the top rung of the ladder. When LLMs keep failing on a mechanical task, apply the fix manually; capture as input for routing policy.
4. Measurement feeds optimization. Track per-bead which model attempted, how long, whether it passed review, what failure mode if any.

## Existing bead tie-ins (helix review-loop epic from parallel agent)

- ddx-a317208c exec: post-merge review agent on-by-default in execute-loop
- ddx-dc231144 exec: tier-based auto-escalation driven by resource discovery
- ddx-fdf031c9 exec: ddx bead review <id> — template a review prompt
- ddx-44084b3e exec: ddx bead reopen <id> — atomic review-driven reopen
- ddx-518b2690 exec: ddx agent run --output text — plain result extraction

## Existing bead tie-ins (mine)

- ddx-bd0691fe Epic: surface ddx-agent routing visibility (providers/models/check/route-status commands — prereq for routing policy)
- ddx-bfc1aeaf Visibility 8: capture routing decisions in bead evidence (kind:routing) — feeds the measurement database

## New child beads (to be filed under this epic)

The beads below are scope for this epic. File as children when we are ready to work each one. NOT pre-filed to avoid blocking the current Svelte+GraphQL migration.

1. Per-bead cost aggregation: extend bead evidence schema to record tokens-in/tokens-out/cost-usd per attempt; add ddx bead metrics aggregate command that rolls up per-bead totals.
2. Ladder success-rate analytics: ddx agent metrics tier-success — query last N closed beads, report success rate at each tier (vidar-coder, claude-sonnet, claude-opus, deterministic-fix).
3. Prompt-context pre-filter: shrink the prompt for cheap-tier attempts — omit the full plan doc and include only the bead description + acceptance + 2 most relevant files.
4. Failure-mode taxonomy: document the known failure modes (vidar long-context hang, read-loop stall, ship-unbuilt-code, opus-shape-miss, etc.) as bead evidence kinds so measurement can aggregate by mode.
5. Review-rejection telemetry: aggregate opus-review outcomes (APPROVE / APPROVE_WITH_EDITS / REJECT) per originating tier so we know where the shape gaps are.
6. Dashboard page for the metrics (requires Svelte migration Stage 4 to land first).

## Why this is captured as an epic not a memory-only goal

Memory is read; epics are tracked. Future sessions should see this in ddx bead list and be able to file child beads against it as we discover new measurement needs or optimization opportunities. The memory file is the principle; this epic is the backlog.
title: Epic: cost-tiered execution + measurement — maximize closed-bead throughput per dollar

The workflow should trust the agent to make progress or signal when it's stuck.

acceptance:
AC1. When an execute-bead session exits without committing in the worktree AND without writing .ddx/executions/<id>/no_changes_rationale.txt, the harness records a NEW dedicated status (e.g. 'agent_silent_exit' or 'no_evidence_produced') instead of letting ddx-land-coordinator land an iteration containing only execution evidence.

AC2. The codex reviewer's BLOCK rationale ('change set only adds execution metadata files') becomes redundant — the harness catches this case before the reviewer is even invoked, saving a redundant review pass and its cost.

AC3. The execute-bead prompt template is updated so the rationale-on-incomplete-work instruction is harder to skip — ideally a tool the agent must invoke (e.g. SubmitNoChangesRationale tool) rather than 'write a file and exit'.

AC4. When the agent did run Edit tool calls but the worktree has no commit, the harness logs a warning naming each file the agent edited that is now uncommitted, so operators can recover the work or diagnose the silent-commit-failure.

AC5. Documentation in docs/ explains the agent-self-commit contract, the rationale-file fallback, and the new 'agent_silent_exit' classification.
description:
Observed today across 5 attempts on the axon repo (claude/sonnet, claude/opus, codex/gpt-5.4-mini): the agent does real work in the worktree (multiple Edit tool calls touching real source files) but never runs git commit. Result: iteration commit is built by ddx-land-coordinator with ONLY execution-evidence files (.ddx/executions/<id>/result.json, manifest.json, usage.json), no actual code or doc changes. The post-merge codex reviewer correctly flags BLOCK with rationale 'change set only adds execution metadata files'.

Concrete evidence (axon repo, 2026-04-29):

  Attempt: 20260429T235353-d14f6b91 (axon-80979cb8 / A1 sub-bead)
  Harness: claude / model: sonnet
  Duration: 817 sec, cost $3.61, 132 tool calls
  Tool breakdown: 79 Bash, 34 Read, 11 Grep, 8 Edit
  Edit targets: ui/src/lib/api.ts, ui/src/lib/policy-evaluator.ts,
    ui/src/routes/.../policies/+page.svelte, ui/tests/e2e/link-policy-retry.spec.ts
  Iteration commit (ddx-land-coordinator): contains exactly 2 files —
    .ddx/executions/20260429T235353-d14f6b91/result.json (23 lines)
    .ddx/executions/20260429T235353-d14f6b91/usage.json (9 lines)
  Net: zero actual edits landed despite $3.61 of model time spent making them.

The same pattern appeared on:
  20260429T235406-20eeaf2c (axon-db7a6d0a, claude/sonnet, $0.57, 4.2 min, BLOCK)
  20260429T190657-fa91f49b (axon-ab2e52e0, openrouter/gpt-5.4-mini, $0.08, 24s, stalled)
  20260429T190634-579df880 (axon-80979cb8, openrouter/gpt-5.4-mini, $0.12, 104s, BLOCK)
  20260429T190646-5aa5b46e (axon-db7a6d0a, openrouter/gpt-5.4-mini, $0.06, 25s, BLOCK)

Total burn today: ~$4.50 across 5 attempts on 3 beads, 0 closed.

The execute-bead prompt template tells the agent to commit:
  '4. Commit once, when everything is green. Stage only the files you intentionally changed with git add <specific-paths>...'

So agent self-commit is the contract. But:

(a) When typecheck/lint/test fails, the agent is told to fix or write no_changes_rationale.txt — but apparently many agents are bailing silently without writing the rationale either, so the harness sees a clean worktree (no diff staged or committed) and treats it as 'agent made no commits'. The iteration then carries only the auto-generated execution evidence.

(b) There may also be a path where the agent commits but a pre-commit hook silently rejects it. Have not confirmed; the worktree was reaped before I could inspect.

The issue spans multiple harnesses (claude, codex via embedded openrouter), so it is not specific to one provider's commit semantics. Either:
  - The harness should reject 'agent made no commits AND no no_changes_rationale.txt' as a separate failure class instead of silently letting ddx-land-coordinator land an empty iteration
  - The agent prompt should make rationale-writing on incomplete work non-optional with stronger language
  - The harness should run a final-stage check 'did the agent produce ANY signal — commit OR rationale — and if not, treat as harness-execution-failed and surface to the operator'
title: execute-bead: agents make edits but skip git commit; iteration commits land only execution evidence

acceptance: ddx server workers log shows real-time progress (turn count, last tool, elapsed) during a claude-harness execute-bead run; operators do not need to poll ps or inspect worktrees to confirm the agent is alive and making progress
description:
The claude harness runs with --print mode which buffers all output until completion. ddx server workers log shows zero lines during execution — no tool calls, no turn counts, no timing — leaving operators blind for 20-40 minutes per bead.

The ddx-agent harness emits per-tool-call progress lines (→ llm request, ← llm response, 🔧 tool) that the worker log surfaces in real time. The claude harness should do the same.

Options:
1. Switch claude harness to --output-format stream-json and parse events as they arrive, writing progress to a sidecar file in the execution dir
2. Write a progress sidecar file the worker log command tails
3. Surface: turn count, last tool called, elapsed time, token estimate within 30s of each LLM response
title: Stream real-time progress from claude harness during execute-bead

acceptance: On a host with a reachable lmstudio-protocol provider (e.g., bragi or vidar), running 'ddx work --local --once --harness lmstudio --model qwen3.5-27b' against any ready bead produces a result.json with duration_ms > 0 and either exit_code=0 or a detail that names a real LLM-side failure (timeout, HTTP error, provider 5xx). 'exec: no command' with duration_ms=0 MUST NOT appear in any execute-loop result for a harness whose Surface="embedded-openai". A Go test in cli/internal/agent/ covers this by constructing an lmstudio candidate with Binary="" and asserts the dispatch picks the embedded-openai code path, not the exec-a-binary code path.
description:
Follow-on to ddx-1cfce460 (closed). The four recent routing fixes (55cbd50d, bb11d89d, 3598d014, bfcb7a61) resolved Symptom 1 (catalog→provider resolution). Symptom 2 (lmstudio candidate fails at exec) still reproduces in the installed binary (v0.6.0-alpha21 / commit 9436b58c built 2026-04-17T20:16Z).

Reproducer on host 'eitri' (bragi + vidar both healthy per 'ddx agent check'):
1. Start 'ddx work' on a project with a ready bead and no pinned --harness/--model.
2. Cheap-tier candidate is evaluated first.
3. Routing event logs: {"resolved_provider":"lmstudio","resolved_model":"qwen3.5-27b","fallback_chain":[]}
4. Tier-attempt event logs: tier=cheap harness=lmstudio model=qwen3.5-27b probe=ok, exec: no command
5. result.json shows:
   harness=lmstudio, model=qwen3.5-27b,
   status=execution_failed, detail="exec: no command",
   duration_ms=0, exit_code=-1, failure_mode=unknown
6. DDx escalates past standard (codex/gpt-5.4 also bails) and ends up at smart tier (claude-opus-4-6), which succeeds. Cheap tier burns <1s but produces no inference and no useful diagnostic.

Root cause: registry.go:140-146 defines lmstudio with Binary="", Surface="embedded-openai", IsHTTPProvider=true. routing.go:202 has an explicit embedded-openai case for the lmstudio harness. But the exec dispatch layer is still treating lmstudio as an exec-a-binary harness, and with Binary="" it produces the zero-duration "exec: no command" error instead of routing through the embedded-openai surface.

Evidence preserved at:
- /Users/erik/Projects/helix/.ddx/executions/20260417T214423-ed7adc95/{manifest,result}.json (cheap-tier attempt on helix-00e2486f)

Why this matters: every execute-loop run on this host silently wastes the cheap tier and escalates directly to the smart tier. The discovery work in 55cbd50d shipped on a routing-decision acceptance criterion without an execution-success acceptance, so the scoring layer correctly picks lmstudio but the dispatch layer cannot serve it — a hollow close on ddx-71fe194f's acceptance.

Related: the helix-side integration harness bead (helix-3fdba193) should add a cheap-tier-actually-executes assertion to prevent this class of regression from reappearing.
title: lmstudio harness candidate routes correctly but fails at exec with "exec: no command"

Generate two classes of visuals:

A) TECHNICAL DIAGRAMS (SVG or Mermaid, committed alongside docs):
- Three-layer stack: DDx (platform) ↔ HELIX (workflow) ↔ Dun (quality). Show how a workflow tool consumes DDx primitives.
- Bead lifecycle: open → ready → in_progress → closed (with review, push-conflict, declined branches).
- Execute-loop flow: claim bead → render prompt → run agent → review → merge/push (auto-recover on race) → close.
- Persona/role binding: roles in workflow (e.g. code-reviewer) → persona definitions in library → project binding in .ddx/config.yaml.
- Project-local install model: <projectRoot>/.ddx/plugins/<plugin>/, <projectRoot>/.agents/skills/, <projectRoot>/.claude/skills/ all real-files; ddx-server is the only global artifact.

Place SVGs at website/static/diagrams/<name>.svg or use Mermaid code blocks inside markdown. Wire them into the microsite concept pages where V2 left placeholders.

B) HERO GRAPHIC (raster, polished, for the microsite landing page):
- Use the nano-banana-pro-openrouter skill to generate a hero image.
- Subject: visual metaphor for document-driven development with AI agents. Suggest: a structured document tree feeding into agent workflows, not generic 'AI' clip art. The user explicitly wants something distinctive, not generic AI aesthetic.
- 2K output minimum. Multiple variants OK; pick the strongest.
- Place at website/static/hero/landing.png (or .webp for size).
- Wire into website/content/_index.md or wherever the landing hero is rendered.

PREREQUISITES:
- Set OPENROUTER_API_KEY in env or .env to use the nano-banana skill.
- If the API key isn't available, this bead creates the SVG diagrams and prepares the prompt + integration point for the hero, then declines the hero portion with a clear blocker note.

OUT OF SCOPE: animations, video, anything interactive. Static SVG/PNG only.

acceptance:
- [ ] --output flag accepts text | json-result | session-jsonl
- [ ] Default is text
- [ ] Integration test covers all three modes
- [ ] No behavior change for session-jsonl mode
description:
ddx agent run currently prints the agent's full session (JSONL events) to stdout regardless of flags. To extract just the final assistant text message, callers have to parse JSONL and walk the session log. This breaks trivial automation like 'review=$(ddx agent run ... --prompt @/tmp/r.md)'.

## Scope
- --output text: emit only the final assistant text message to stdout (no tool calls, no JSONL)
- --output json-result: emit only the result-type JSON object (session metadata + final text)
- --output session-jsonl: current behavior; keep as non-default opt-in
- Default switches to --output text

## Context
Observed during axon ADR-018 loop: both review runs spilled 90-200KB of session JSONL to stdout, requiring python3 JSON-walking to extract the ~2KB opus verdict. --output text would have made the review invocation one line instead of three.
title: exec: ddx agent run --output text — plain result extraction

description:
Finding from fresh-eyes review of the run/try/work boundary plan.

Problem:
The plan says there should be one simple run command layered under try and work, but current governing docs and shipped skill references still describe ddx agent run, ddx agent execute-bead, execute-loop, quorum harness flags, and agent-provider command surfaces as active user/automation primitives. Examples found during review include FEAT-006 Agent Service overview/responsibilities, FEAT-010 Layer 1 wording, and cli/internal/skills/ddx/reference/agents.md persona/quorum/help examples. Parallel copied skill docs under .agents/skills/ddx and .claude/skills/ddx contain the same stale vocabulary.

Why this matters:
The docs are executable guidance for agents. Leaving legacy command names and flags in skills will keep agents routing work through the old surfaces even if the implementation adds top-level run/try/work.

Required fix:
Make the public command taxonomy bright-line and uniform across feature docs, skill docs, and CLI help: ddx run is the single agent execution primitive, ddx try layers retry/review policy over run, and ddx work layers bead orchestration over try. Agent-internal service commands may exist only as hidden/internal plumbing and must not be documented as workflow commands.

Acceptance:
- FEAT-006 and FEAT-010 no longer describe ddx agent run/execute-bead/execute-loop as public orchestration commands.
- DDx skill references in cli/internal/skills/ddx, .agents/skills/ddx, and .claude/skills/ddx use ddx run/try/work consistently.
- Quorum/compare/persona examples are either migrated to the simplified surface or removed if they imply DDx harness routing.
- A docs/skill grep test or validation step fails on stale public references to ddx agent run, execute-bead, execute-loop, --harnesses, or provider-routing examples outside explicit migration notes.
title: review finding: remove stale ddx agent run and execute-bead command guidance

acceptance: The DDx docs define `ddx agent execute-bead` as a clean-worktree workspace transform from `W` to `W'`; the bead is specified as the executable transform contract rather than a vague tracker ticket; DDx docs no longer assign governing-artifact resolution or prompt construction to DDx at runtime; a generic bead execution-readiness validation surface is specified with clear DDx-owned vs HELIX-owned checks; the docs define the success/failure state machine including structural validation, required post-run checks, preserve-on-failure evidence, and rebase+fast-forward landing on success; and the contract explicitly remains valid for future multi-project `ddx server` worker pools rather than assuming a single served repo
description:
<context-digest>
Review area: execute-bead semantics, executable bead contracts, readiness validation, and DDx/HELIX boundary ownership. Evidence covers FEAT-006, FEAT-010, FEAT-013, SD-012, server-driven execution discussions, and cli/cmd/agent_execute_bead.go.
</context-digest>
Define DDx bead execution around the functional model `W' = execute(bead, W)`, where `W` is a clean git-backed workspace input state and `ddx agent execute-bead` materializes that state in an isolated worktree before mutation.

## Required design outcomes
1. Clarify that `ddx agent execute-bead` starts from a resolved base revision, treats the caller's dirty worktree as outside the transform, checkpoints it separately, and executes only against the isolated worktree state.
2. Define the bead as the executable transform contract: the bead must carry the instruction/prompt, acceptance, declared context inputs, execution policy, landing/preservation policy, and post-run validation policy needed for replayable execution.
3. Unwind DDx designs that currently imply DDx derives governing artifacts, prompt construction, or workflow policy from the live document graph at execution time. DDx should provide composable graph/exec tools, not hidden workflow orchestration.
4. Define a generic DDx execution-readiness validation surface for beads, such as `ddx bead validate --execution` or equivalent inspection semantics, that checks structural executability without embedding HELIX-specific policy.
5. Specify the success/failure state machine around execution: structural validation, agent execution, post-run validity checks / required executions, preserve-on-failure evidence semantics, and land-on-success semantics.
6. Make the automation boundary explicit: future humans, HELIX flows, or `ddx server` worker pools must consume the explicit bead contract and validator output rather than relying on hidden prompt assembly or runtime artifact inference.
7. Preserve room for multi-project server execution: the contract must not assume a single served repo or a single server process, and must remain valid when project-scoped worktrees are managed by a multi-project `ddx server`.
8. Make HELIX's responsibility explicit: HELIX should author the executable bead contract and create a new contract document in `docs/helix/02-design/contracts/` that defines the DDx/HELIX boundary for executable bead execution. The contracts directory does not currently exist in this repo, so this work must establish it.

## Spec and design scope
- Reconcile FEAT-006 agent-service workflow with the workspace-transform model
- Reconcile FEAT-010 execution-definition language so execution selection and required checks are explicit input or separately composed query output, not hidden bead-context derivation
- Preserve FEAT-013's substrate-not-orchestration boundary while still supporting async server-managed worker pools
- Align SD-012 git-awareness wording with the actual clean-input/worktree model, checkpoint semantics, and preserve-vs-land outcomes
- Establish how the generic execution-readiness surface is consumed by future automation without embedding HELIX policy in DDx

## Expected artifact work
- New contract under `docs/helix/02-design/contracts/` for the executable bead boundary
- Updates to the affected feature / solution design docs so they all describe the same execution contract
- Follow-on design beads for server-managed supervision and multi-project server topology
- Follow-on implementation beads only after the contract is settled
title: Define execute-bead workspace-transform contract and readiness validation

acceptance:
AC1. ResolveTierModelRef (or its replacement) consults the model catalog as the default source of tier→model mapping when agent.routing.model_overrides is not set, returning the first surface→model pair whose harness is available and whose model resolves to a healthy provider.

AC2. `ddx agent execute-loop --once --local --no-adaptive-min-tier` succeeds against a fresh project (no model_overrides in .ddx/config.yaml) when the catalog has at least one tier with a reachable harness/model/provider chain.

AC3. When EVERY catalog surface for the resolved tier is unreachable, the tier-attempt event records the concrete reason for each surface tried (e.g. 'codex: harness not installed', 'claude: provider cooldown', 'embedded-openai: model qwen3.6 has no reachable provider'), not a generic 'no viable harness found' with empty fields.

AC4. `ddx agent doctor` (or `ddx agent catalog show`) emits a warning when a tier's catalog entries reference a model id that has no matching named route AND no provider /v1/models entry across configured providers, so the user discovers the gap before invoking execute-loop.

AC5. Per-project agent.routing.model_overrides remains a supported override that wins over catalog defaults — backwards compatible.
description:
When agent.routing.model_overrides is not set in a project's .ddx/config.yaml, ResolveTierModelRef in cli/internal/agent/profile_ladder.go:44 returns the literal tier name ("cheap", "standard", "smart") as the model identifier. ResolveRoute is then called with model="cheap"/"standard"/"smart" — strings that match no model id and no named route. Every tier resolves to an empty harness/empty model and is recorded as a tier-attempt with body "no viable harness found". The loop exhausts all tiers and reports "execute-loop: all tiers exhausted — no viable provider found".

This is happening today on a workstation that has bragi/vidar/hel hosting qwen3.6, plus working claude and codex harnesses. execute-bead succeeds with the same providers when --harness/--model are pinned explicitly, proving the underlying routing is fine. Only the tier→model resolution path is broken.

The model catalog (~/.ddx/model-catalog.yaml or built-in defaults, surfaced via `ddx agent catalog show`) ALREADY contains the tier→surface→model mapping that would make this work. The resolver just doesn't consume it. Surfaces and concrete models are listed per tier (e.g. cheap: embedded-openai→qwen3.5-27b, codex→gpt-5.4-mini, claude→claude-haiku-4-5) — the resolver should walk these surfaces in order, picking the first whose harness is healthy and whose model resolves to a reachable provider via model_routes. Per-project model_overrides should be an OPTIONAL override layered on top of catalog defaults, not the only thing that makes tier resolution work.

Caller-side workaround: every project must add agent.routing.model_overrides to .ddx/config.yaml before execute-loop will function. This is silent: there is no diagnostic from `ddx agent doctor`, no warning from `ddx agent catalog show`, and no hint in the "no viable provider" error pointing at the missing config. Users have to read source to understand why a fully-healthy provider/harness fleet produces zero viable tiers.

Repro:
1. Fresh project with .ddx/config.yaml lacking agent.routing.model_overrides
2. Healthy provider config in ~/.config/agent/config.yaml (any qwen/claude/codex)
3. `ddx agent execute-loop --once --local --no-adaptive-min-tier`
4. Every tier-attempt event records "no viable harness found"; loop exits with 'all tiers exhausted'

Related symptom — the adaptive min-tier promotion uses cheap-tier success rate to skip cheap, but with the bug above no cheap-tier attempt has ever produced a successful harness invocation, so the trailing window is biased to 0% by design rather than by actual model performance.
title: execute-loop: tier resolver should consume catalog instead of requiring per-project model_overrides

acceptance: Concurrent execute-bead workers no longer checkpoint unrelated DDx runtime churn from other active attempts into their base revisions; the snapshot boundary for DDx-owned files is documented and enforced; and concurrency tests cover the reproduced multi-worker case.
description:
<context-digest>
Observed while running multiple execute-loop workers concurrently on 2026-04-11. Replacing git stash with checkpoint commits fixed the immediate beads.lock/workers race, but execute-bead still starts from a dirty repo root where .ddx/beads.jsonl and other DDx-owned execution artifacts may be changing because of other live workers. That means one attempt can inherit unrelated DDx runtime/evidence churn into its checkpoint base.
</context-digest>
Tighten execute-bead checkpointing so an attempt's input snapshot is not polluted by unrelated DDx-generated state from other live executions.

## Scope
- Define which DDx-owned paths are part of the execution input snapshot vs excluded runtime churn
- Prevent unrelated live tracker/evidence/worker mutations from being captured into a new attempt base by default
- Add concurrency-focused tests covering simultaneous workers and dirty DDx-owned state
- Keep the tracked execution-evidence contract coherent with the new snapshot boundary
title: Keep execute-bead checkpoint bases free of unrelated DDx runtime churn
